proposals.title: A National Registry for Surgical Incision Technique Outcomes in Otolaryngology: A Feasibility Study and Pilot Analysis for Chronic Rhinosinusitis Procedures
proposals.motivation: Surgical incision techniques, such as those used in endoscopic sinus surgery for chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS), are critical determinants of patient outcomes, including infection risk, healing time, and scar formation. Current evidence is limited to single-center, retrospective studies with small sample sizes, leading to a lack of consensus on optimal techniques. A population-level registry, as proposed here, is urgently needed to generate high-quality comparative effectiveness data to guide surgical practice and reduce post-operative complications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a national, multi-institutional registry for otolaryngologic surgical incision techniques is feasible to implement and will reveal significant, previously undetected associations between specific technical variations (e.g., blade type, incision length/location) and key post-operative outcomes, including surgical site infection rates and patient-reported healing.
proposals.method: We will develop a secure, web-based registry platform using a cloud-based architecture (AWS or Azure) with HIPAA-compliant data storage. The data model will be co-designed with clinicians to capture structured intraoperative variables (technique, instrumentation) linked to outcomes from electronic health records (EHRs) via standardized APIs (e.g., FHIR). A pilot will be conducted across 5-10 academic otolaryngology departments. We will employ natural language processing (NLP) algorithms, specifically a BERT-based model fine-tuned on operative notes, to automate the extraction of incision details from unstructured surgical reports to ensure scalability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first proof-of-concept for a national surgical technique registry in otolaryngology, providing a scalable model for outcomes research. It will generate the first population-level evidence on the real-world effectiveness of different incision approaches for CRS and similar procedures, directly informing surgical training and practice guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While disease registries exist, a registry focused specifically on granular surgical *technique* as the primary exposure variable is novel. The integration of automated NLP for technique extraction from operative notes moves beyond manual data entry, addressing a major scalability barrier for such detailed procedural registries.

proposals.title: Disentangling Surgical Risk Factors: A Multivariate Analysis of Incision Tension, Flap Design, and Tracheostoma Incorporation on Pharyngocutaneous Fistula Formation
proposals.motivation: Pharyngocutaneous fistula (PCF) is a major complication after total laryngectomy, leading to prolonged hospitalization, delayed adjuvant therapy, and increased morbidity. The common surgical practice of incorporating the tracheostoma into the primary neck incision is widely assumed to increase PCF risk, but this association may be confounded by unmeasured technical factors like incision tension and flap design. Clarifying the true drivers of dehiscence is critical for refining surgical technique and improving patient outcomes in head and neck oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the elevated risk of PCF attributed to tracheostoma incorporation is not causal but is instead confounded by increased local incision tension and specific flap designs required when creating a combined incision. The primary independent risk factors for dehiscence are mechanical tension and vascular compromise, not the presence of the stoma itself.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients who underwent total laryngectomy (2015-2024). Primary variables will be surgical technique (tracheostoma incorporated vs. separate), quantified incision tension via pre-closure photographic measurement using a validated digital image analysis algorithm (e.g., measuring wound edge distraction), and flap design (e.g., apron vs. utility). A multivariate logistic regression model will assess the independent association of each variable with PCF, controlling for known confounders (e.g., prior radiotherapy, albumin, diabetes). Sensitivity analyses will evaluate effect modification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence separating the mechanical and technical factors in laryngectomy closure from the simple binary of stoma incorporation. It will directly inform surgical decision-making, potentially validating safer techniques that incorporate the stoma without increasing tension. The findings will refine the 'inventory' of modifiable risk factors for this costly post-operative 'infection' and 'disease' complication.
proposals.novelty: While the association between incorporated stoma and fistula is clinical dogma, no prior study has attempted to objectively measure and statistically control for the confounding effects of incision tension and flap geometry, moving beyond a simplistic causal attribution. This approach applies a 'diagnostic' rigor to a long-held surgical assumption.

proposals.title: Cognitive Reserve and Comorbidity Burden Outperform Chronological Age as Predictors of Cochlear Implant Outcomes in Older Adults: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implantation in older adults is frequently delayed or denied based on chronological age, despite growing evidence that elderly patients can achieve significant audiologic benefit. Current candidacy guidelines and prognostic models overweight age as a standalone predictor, potentially denying intervention to cognitively robust older patients while inadequately screening for modifiable risk factors. Disentangling the independent contributions of cognitive reserve, comorbidity burden, and chronological age is essential for equitable, evidence-based candidacy decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: Cognitive reserve (measured by validated proxy indices including education, occupational complexity, and leisure activity engagement) and cumulative comorbidity burden (quantified by the Charlson Comorbidity Index) will each independently predict post-implantation speech perception outcomes with greater effect sizes than chronological age alone, and a composite model incorporating these factors will significantly outperform age-only prognostic models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study enrolling 200 adults aged 60 and older receiving their first cochlear implant across three academic medical centers. At baseline, participants will complete the Cognitive Reserve Index questionnaire, the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), and the Charlson Comorbidity Index, alongside standard audiometric evaluation. Primary outcomes will be AzBio sentence scores in quiet and noise at 6 and 12 months post-activation. We will fit hierarchical linear regression models with age, cognitive reserve, and comorbidity burden as predictors, using likelihood-ratio tests and Akaike Information Criterion to compare nested models. Random forest and elastic net regression will serve as secondary analyses to capture nonlinear interactions and identify the most influential predictor subsets.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, multi-site evidence quantifying the relative predictive power of cognitive reserve and comorbidity burden versus chronological age for cochlear implant outcomes. The resulting composite prognostic model can directly inform clinical candidacy evaluation, reducing age-based bias in implantation decisions. Findings will support more personalized preoperative counseling and may expand access for cognitively healthy older adults currently excluded by age-centric criteria.
proposals.novelty: While prior retrospective studies have examined age or cognition in isolation, no prospective multi-site study has simultaneously modeled cognitive reserve indices, cumulative comorbidity, and age within a single predictive framework for CI outcomes, nor directly tested whether age loses significance when these confounders are properly controlled.

proposals.title: Bidirectional Cascades Between Hearing Loss, Social Isolation, and Chronic Disease: A Longitudinal Cross-Lagged Panel Analysis
proposals.motivation: The prevailing clinical model frames hearing loss as a unidirectional trigger leading sequentially to social isolation, cognitive decline, and poorer general health. However, emerging evidence suggests that systemic inflammatory diseases, depression, and social disengagement may themselves accelerate auditory decline, implying a cyclical rather than linear cascade. Clarifying directionality is critical for designing interventions that break the cycle at multiple entry points rather than targeting hearing rehabilitation alone.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between hearing loss, social isolation, and chronic health deterioration operates as a bidirectional, reinforcing cycle, such that poor general health and social withdrawal predict subsequent worsening of hearing function with effect sizes comparable to those of hearing loss predicting isolation and health decline.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the UK Biobank cohort (N > 150,000 participants with audiometric data), extracting repeated measures of pure-tone audiometry, validated social isolation indices (frequency of social contact, loneliness scales), and chronic disease burden (cardiovascular disease, diabetes, depression diagnoses) across three time points spanning approximately 5-8 years. Cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) and random-intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPM) will be fitted to estimate bidirectional temporal effects while separating within-person change from stable between-person differences. Mediation analyses using structural equation modeling will test whether inflammatory biomarkers (CRP, cytokines) and depressive symptoms serve as mechanistic intermediaries in the reverse pathway from chronic disease to hearing decline. Models will be adjusted for age, sex, noise exposure history, ototoxic medication use, and socioeconomic status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale quantitative evidence for or against bidirectionality in the hearing loss-isolation-health cascade, directly informing whether clinical guidelines should expand beyond hearing-first interventions. If confirmed, the cyclical model would justify integrated care pathways combining audiological rehabilitation with social prescribing and chronic disease management, potentially reducing healthcare costs across multiple specialties.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined pairwise associations between hearing loss and individual outcomes, no study has simultaneously modeled all three nodes of the cascade with methods that can distinguish true bidirectional causation from stable confounding, making this the first rigorous test of cyclicity versus unidirectionality in this clinically important pathway.

proposals.title: A Comorbidity-Informed Framework for Personalizing Cochlear Implant Rehabilitation in Older Adults: A Pilot Study Integrating Clinical Data and Remote Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implant (CI) outcomes in older adults are highly variable, partly due to heterogeneous comorbidity burdens (e.g., cognitive decline, diabetes, chronic rhinosinusitis) that affect rehabilitation capacity. Pre-2026, rehabilitation protocols are largely one-size-fits-all, failing to account for these critical individual differences in disease and functional status, potentially leading to suboptimal outcomes and resource inefficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stratifying older CI recipients by their comorbidity profiles and baseline functional status will allow for the design of tailored rehabilitation protocols that yield superior adherence and auditory performance outcomes compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective pilot study enrolling older adult CI candidates. Pre-implant, we will use a structured inventory (e.g., Charlson Comorbidity Index, Montreal Cognitive Assessment) to create comorbidity-functional clusters via hierarchical clustering. Post-implant, participants will be randomized to a standard protocol or a tailored protocol (e.g., modified session length/frequency, integrated cognitive exercises, remote support via video check-ins for those with high infection risk or mobility issues). Outcome measures (speech perception, quality of life, adherence tracked via a clinician-facing dashboard) will be compared at 3 and 6 months using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for personalizing CI aural rehabilitation based on objective comorbidity data. It will contribute a practical, data-driven methodology for clinicians to optimize post-operative care pathways, potentially improving cost-effectiveness and patient-centered outcomes in a growing, complex patient population.
proposals.novelty: While comorbidity management is recognized, its systematic integration into the structure and delivery of CI rehabilitation protocols is novel. The approach leverages existing clinical inventories and remote monitoring (video) – tools accelerated by COVID-19-era telemedicine – specifically for personalization in otology, moving beyond diagnostic categorization to active therapeutic adaptation.

proposals.title: A Prospective Cohort Study: Integrating a Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA) into Cochlear Implant Candidacy Evaluation to Predict Postoperative Functional and Audiologic Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implant (CI) candidacy is expanding to include older, more medically complex adults, yet standard audiologic and medical evaluations may not fully capture the geriatric-specific factors (e.g., frailty, cognitive reserve, polypharmacy, social support) that critically influence surgical risk, rehabilitation adherence, and functional benefit. This gap leads to suboptimal patient selection and outcome prediction. Applying a structured, multidisciplinary CGA, a cornerstone of geriatric medicine pre-2026, offers a novel framework to address this.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deficits identified in a pre-operative CGA, particularly in domains of physical frailty, cognitive function, and social support, will be independently associated with poorer post-CI outcomes, including slower auditory rehabilitation, smaller gains in speech perception, and reduced quality-of-life improvement, after controlling for standard audiologic and medical covariates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of 150 CI candidates aged ≥70 years. Pre-operatively, participants will undergo a standardized CGA administered by a study clinician, assessing frailty (Fried phenotype), cognition (MoCA), nutrition (MNA), mood (GDS), functional status (ADLs/IADLs), and social support. Primary outcomes (at 6 and 12 months post-activation) will include objective speech perception scores (AzBio in quiet/noise) and patient-reported outcome measures (NCIQ, HUI-3). We will use multivariable linear and logistic regression models to test associations between CGA domain scores and outcomes, adjusting for age, hearing duration, and preoperative speech scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, geriatric-specific assessment protocol for CI candidacy, moving beyond chronologic age. It will generate evidence-based criteria to identify older adults at high risk for poor outcomes who may benefit from pre-habilitation or tailored counseling. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines and shared decision-making for a growing patient population.
proposals.novelty: While CGA is standard in oncology and cardiology, its application to CI candidacy is novel. This proposal integrates core geriatric principles directly into otologic practice, creating a new predictive model that synthesizes biologic, cognitive, and social determinants of success, which are not captured by existing audiologic batteries.

proposals.title: A Population-Level Markov Model for the Cost-Effectiveness of Cochlear Implantation in Aging Societies: Integrating Otologic Infection Risk and Social Determinants
proposals.motivation: The global population is aging, leading to a projected increase in age-related hearing loss and associated social isolation, cognitive decline, and healthcare costs. Pre-2026, cost-effectiveness analyses (CEA) for cochlear implants (CIs) are typically individual-level, failing to capture population dynamics and system-wide financial impacts. Furthermore, untreated hearing loss may complicate management of chronic conditions prevalent in the elderly, such as chronic rhinosinusitis, and increase vulnerability to social determinants of health, creating a critical evidence gap for policymakers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population-level Markov model, incorporating risks of otologic infections and variations in social determinants (e.g., living situation, access to clinicians), will demonstrate that expanded CI provision is a cost-effective public health intervention for aging societies, with savings accruing from reduced downstream medical and social care costs.
proposals.method: We will construct a probabilistic, state-transition Markov model simulating a closed elderly cohort. Health states will include: unaided hearing loss, CI candidacy, post-implantation (with states for successful use, device failure, and post-operative infection), and death. Transition probabilities and costs (device, surgery, rehabilitation, infection treatment) will be derived from pre-2026 meta-analyses and Medicare claims data. The model will be scaled using U.S. Census projections. Sensitivity analyses will test the impact of varying infection rates (informed by recent otologic literature) and parameters linked to social determinants (e.g., differential access to follow-up care). The primary outcome is the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) at the population level.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first health-economic model of CI provision that operates at a population scale, explicitly integrating clinical risks (infection) and non-clinical factors influencing outcomes. It will deliver a policy-relevant tool for governments and insurers (e.g., Medicare) to project long-term budgetary impacts and health benefits of hearing rehabilitation, directly addressing recent calls for insurer-involved clinical research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling CI CEA from the individual to the demographic population level and systematically integrating otologic infection risk as a key cost driver, a factor often omitted from prior economic models, thereby creating a more realistic simulation for public health decision-making.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Cohort Study of Cochlear Implantation and Cognitive Trajectories: Investigating Early Intervention as a Modifier of Age-Related Decline
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes a strong association between age-related hearing loss (ARHL) and accelerated cognitive decline/dementia risk, framing ARHL as a major modifiable risk factor. However, the critical inverse question—whether early intervention with cochlear implantation (CI) can actively prevent or slow this decline—remains inadequately tested. This gap limits evidence-based clinical guidance for otologists and audiologists counseling patients on the potential cognitive benefits of CI beyond auditory rehabilitation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adults with qualifying ARHL who undergo cochlear implantation earlier in their diagnostic and treatment pathway will demonstrate significantly slower rates of cognitive decline over a 5-year follow-up period compared to matched controls receiving standard auditory care (hearing aids).
proposals.method: We propose a prospective, longitudinal matched-cohort study. Participants (n=200, age 60-75) with moderate-to-severe ARHL will be recruited: an Intervention Arm (CI recipients) and a Control Arm (hearing aid users), matched for age, baseline cognition (MoCA), and cardiovascular risk. The primary outcome is the change in a comprehensive neuropsychological test battery administered annually. Secondary outcomes include quality-of-life inventories (HHIE, SSQ) and caregiver reports. We will employ linear mixed-effects models to analyze longitudinal cognitive trajectories, controlling for covariates like baseline diagnosis, incident infections, and vascular disease. Subgroup analyses will assess the influence of pre-implant cognitive reserve.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, controlled evidence directly testing CI as a proactive intervention against cognitive decline, moving beyond cross-sectional association. It will generate a novel dataset linking audiological, cognitive, and patient-reported outcomes to inform clinical decision-making. The findings could reshape the cost-benefit analysis for CI in older adults and influence insurance coverage policies by demonstrating potential long-term health system savings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates hearing loss with dementia, this study uniquely inverts the framework to test CI as a preventive neuroprotective intervention. It leverages Dr. Clark's expertise in diagnosis, disease inventory, and patient-clinician dynamics (e.g., video consultations post-COVID) to design a pragmatic clinical research model that addresses a causal question previously explored only epidemiologically.

proposals.title: A Community Health Worker-Led Framework for Cochlear Implant Candidacy Screening and Post-Operative Rehabilitation in Low-Resource Settings
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implant (CI) rehabilitation is resource-intensive, requiring specialized audiologists and clinicians, creating a profound access gap in developing nations. This disparity disproportionately affects the elderly, where untreated hearing loss accelerates cognitive decline and social isolation. The COVID-19 pandemic demonstrated the utility of remote, task-shifted healthcare delivery, providing a model for scaling complex services where specialist density is low.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, task-shifted framework utilizing trained community health workers (CHWs) and validated remote assessment tools can accurately identify CI candidates and deliver effective, guideline-adherent post-operative rehabilitation in low-resource settings, achieving non-inferior patient outcomes in key auditory and quality-of-life metrics compared to standard specialist-led care.
proposals.method: We will adapt pre-2026 tele-audiology protocols (e.g., digit triplet tests, parent/caregiver-reported outcome inventories) for CHW administration via tablet. A two-stage algorithm will screen for CI candidacy: Stage 1 uses a CHW-collected video of patient response to calibrated sounds and a disease/infection history questionnaire. Stage 2 triggers a secure, asynchronous video review by a remote otolaryngologist. Post-operatively, CHWs will follow a structured video-guided rehabilitation curriculum, with clinician oversight via a centralized dashboard tracking patient progress and flagging complications.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, scalable protocol for CI rehabilitation in resource-limited settings, directly addressing the WHO's focus on integrated ear and hearing care. It will yield a practical toolkit for health systems, including training modules for CHWs, remote monitoring algorithms, and a cost-effectiveness analysis to guide policy and insurance coverage discussions in developing health economies.
proposals.novelty: While tele-audiology exists, this proposal uniquely integrates task-shifting to non-specialists with a structured diagnostic and rehabilitation algorithm specifically for the high-touch CI pathway, moving beyond simple consultation to a full, deployable system of care. It applies lessons from pandemic-era remote management of chronic conditions (like sinusitis) to the distinct challenge of surgical hearing rehabilitation.

proposals.title: Optimizing Restorabite Rehabilitation Protocols for Trismus in Head and Neck Cancer Survivors with Prior Radiotherapy: A Prospective, Video-Assisted Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Trismus, a debilitating restriction of jaw opening, is a common sequela of head and neck cancer (HNC) treatment, particularly radiotherapy (RT), which induces fibrosis. Standardized rehabilitation devices like Restorabite show variable efficacy, with clinical observation suggesting poorer outcomes in the RT subgroup. This creates a critical need for tailored protocols to improve functional recovery and quality of life in this growing survivor population, a pre-2026 clinical priority.
proposals.hypothesis: A modified, intensified Restorabite rehabilitation protocol, incorporating pre-emptive stretching and patient-specific adherence monitoring, will yield superior improvements in maximal interincisal opening (MIO) and patient-reported outcomes compared to the standard protocol in HNC survivors with trismus secondary to radiotherapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of HNC survivors with post-RT trismus (MIO ≤ 35mm). Participants will be enrolled in a modified 12-week protocol featuring a structured pre-habilitation phase and intensified daily exercise duration. Adherence and technique will be monitored via a secure, HIPAA-compliant video diary platform (e.g., a modified ResearchKit framework), where patients upload short, timestamped exercise videos. Clinicians will review videos weekly to provide feedback. Primary outcome is change in MIO; secondary outcomes include the Gothenburg Trismus Questionnaire and analysis of adherence patterns from video metadata.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first structured, evidence-based rehabilitation protocol specifically designed for the challenging RT-associated trismus population. It will establish the feasibility and utility of video-based remote monitoring for improving adherence and clinician oversight in trismus therapy, directly addressing gaps in personalized post-cancer care.
proposals.novelty: While Restorabite is an established tool, the systematic optimization of its application for the distinct pathophysiology of radiation fibrosis is novel. The integration of a secure video diary for real-time adherence tracking and clinician feedback in trismus rehabilitation represents a significant methodological innovation over traditional logbooks or sporadic clinic visits.

proposals.title: Long-Term Efficacy of Early Restorabite Therapy for Post-Infection Trismus: A Prospective Cohort Study with 24-Month Follow-Up
proposals.motivation: Trismus, a debilitating sequela of head and neck infections and treatments, severely impacts quality of life. While early intervention with devices like Restorabite shows promise in short-term studies, the durability of these gains is unknown. This creates uncertainty for clinicians and patients regarding the necessity and timing of maintenance therapy or further interventions, a critical gap in post-acute and chronic disease management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the significant improvements in maximum interincisal opening (MIO) achieved with early Restorabite therapy at 12 months will be maintained at 24 months without additional intensive intervention. We further hypothesize that baseline disease severity and patient adherence will be key predictors of long-term outcome stability.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, longitudinal cohort study extending follow-up from a completed 12-month Restorabite trial. The primary outcome is change in MIO from 12 to 24 months, measured by blinded clinicians using standardized calipers. Secondary outcomes include patient-reported inventories (e.g., Gothenburg Trismus Questionnaire) and adherence logs. We will employ linear mixed-effects models to analyze longitudinal MIO trajectories, controlling for baseline infection severity (e.g., Lund-Mackay score for odontogenic sinusitis) and initial therapy response. Survival analysis (Kaplan-Meier) will assess time to loss of therapeutic gain (defined as MIO regression >5mm).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal data on the durability of a specific trismus therapy beyond one year, directly informing clinical guidelines for monitoring and maintenance. It will establish whether early intervention confers sustained benefit, impacting cost-effectiveness analyses and patient counseling. The analysis of predictors will help create a risk-stratification inventory for long-term management.
proposals.novelty: Existing studies on trismus therapies, including Restorabite, are limited to short-term (≤12 month) follow-up, leaving the natural history of treated trismus undefined. This proposal novelly applies longitudinal modeling from chronic disease research to a post-infection functional sequela, focusing on sustainability of effect rather than just initial efficacy.

proposals.title: Beyond Spoken Words: Quantifying the Impact of Sign Language as a Primary Success Metric in Pediatric Deafness Diagnosis and Management
proposals.motivation: Current diagnostic and intervention frameworks for pediatric deafness, as reflected in parent-reported inventories and clinician assessments, often implicitly prioritize oral language acquisition as the primary success metric. This bias can lead to the undervaluation of sign language development, potentially misclassifying successful linguistic development in signing children as a deficit. This research addresses a critical gap in equitable care, aligning with broader DEI goals in clinical practice by challenging a systemic bias that may disadvantage Deaf children and families.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when sign language fluency is incorporated as a co-primary success metric alongside spoken language in diagnostic and developmental inventories, a significant proportion of children currently categorized as having language delay or disorder will be re-categorized as developing typically within a bimodal-bilingual framework.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using de-identified electronic health record data from pediatric otolaryngology and audiology clinics (pre-2026). We will develop a natural language processing (NLP) pipeline using BERT-based models to extract and classify clinician notes and parent-reported outcomes (e.g., from the MacArthur-Bates Communicative Development Inventories, adapted for sign) for mentions of language modality and developmental concern. A rule-based algorithm will map extracted data to a novel scoring matrix that weights sign and spoken language equally. Statistical analysis (multivariable logistic regression) will compare developmental classifications under the current (oral-privileging) and proposed (dual-pathway) models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence of the prevalence and potential impact of oral-language bias in common clinical assessments. It will contribute a novel, data-driven framework for evaluating language development in deaf children that respects linguistic diversity. The findings will directly inform revisions to parent-report inventories and clinician training, promoting more equitable diagnostic practices.
proposals.novelty: While the value of sign language is recognized, no pre-2026 study has used large-scale clinical data and NLP methods to systematically quantify how its exclusion from primary success metrics alters patient diagnosis and perceived outcomes, directly linking this bias to clinical decision-making pathways.

proposals.title: Divergent Perspectives in Pediatric Upper Respiratory Disease: A Multi-Informant Study of Child Self-Report and Parental Global Ratings for Quality of Life Assessment
proposals.motivation: In pediatric otolaryngology and infectious disease (e.g., chronic rhinosinusitis, recurrent infections), treatment decisions and outcome assessments have historically relied heavily on parent proxy reports. However, pre-2026 literature suggests child and parent perceptions of disease impact, particularly on quality of life (QoL), can differ significantly. Capturing the child's voice is essential for patient-centered care and accurate evaluation of interventions, yet standardized integration of child self-report alongside parental assessments is not routine in clinical research or practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that child self-reported QoL measures will demonstrate significant, clinically meaningful divergence from parental global ratings in pediatric upper respiratory conditions, and that incorporating both perspectives will improve the predictive validity of outcome assessments for disease severity and treatment response.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of children (ages 5-17) with chronic rhinosinusitis or recurrent upper respiratory infections and their parents. Child QoL will be measured using validated, age-appropriate instruments (e.g., the Pediatric Quality of Life Inventory - PedsQL). Parental global ratings will be captured via visual analog scales (VAS) for overall symptom severity and impact. We will employ correlation analyses (Pearson/Spearman), Bland-Altman plots to assess agreement, and multivariate regression modeling to identify factors (e.g., child age, disease duration, parental anxiety) associated with parent-child discrepancy scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence quantifying the divergence between child and parent-reported outcomes in pediatric upper respiratory disease. It will yield a practical framework for integrating multi-informant data in clinical research, directly informing more holistic outcome measures. The findings will advocate for mandatory inclusion of child self-report in future trials and clinical quality-of-life inventories, advancing patient-centered methodologies.
proposals.novelty: While discrepancy is acknowledged, this study systematically quantifies it within otolaryngologic/infectious disease contexts and moves beyond simple correlation to model the determinants of divergence, offering a actionable model for dual-perspective assessment that is not standard in the field.

proposals.title: Socioeconomic Moderators of the Relationship Between Cochlear Implantation Timing and Speech-Language Outcomes in Children With Congenital Hearing Loss
proposals.motivation: Early cochlear implantation is widely recommended to optimize speech and language development in children with severe-to-profound hearing loss, yet outcomes remain highly variable. Prior studies have largely focused on audiological and surgical variables while underexamining how socioeconomic determinants—family income, parental education, and access to rehabilitation—shape the benefit derived from early versus delayed implantation. Understanding these moderators is critical for developing equitable clinical guidelines and targeting support services to families most at risk of suboptimal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lower family income, lower parental education, and reduced access to post-implantation rehabilitation services significantly attenuate the speech-language advantages typically associated with earlier cochlear implantation, such that the critical window effect of implantation age is moderated by socioeconomic status in a dose-dependent manner.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective multicenter cohort study of approximately 400 children who received cochlear implants before age six at three geographically diverse academic otolaryngology centers. Speech-language outcomes will be measured using the Preschool Language Scales (PLS-5) and the Pediatric Speech Intelligibility Test at 12, 24, and 36 months post-activation. Socioeconomic variables—household income bracket, highest parental education level, insurance type, distance to rehabilitation center, and hours of speech-language therapy received—will be collected from medical records and caregiver surveys. Hierarchical linear modeling will be used to test moderation effects, with implantation age as the primary predictor, socioeconomic indicators as moderators, and child-level covariates including etiology, residual hearing, and bilateral versus unilateral implantation. Multiple imputation will address missing data, and sensitivity analyses will examine threshold effects for each socioeconomic variable.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, multicenter evidence quantifying how socioeconomic factors moderate the well-established relationship between implantation timing and pediatric speech-language outcomes. The findings will inform risk-stratified clinical recommendations and identify modifiable barriers—such as therapy access—that, if addressed through targeted interventions or policy changes, could reduce outcome disparities in the cochlear implant population.
proposals.novelty: While individual socioeconomic risk factors have been examined as independent predictors of cochlear implant outcomes, no prior study has formally tested them as moderators of the implantation-timing effect using interaction modeling in a large multicenter design, shifting the clinical question from whether to implant early to for whom early implantation is sufficient without additional socioeconomic support.

proposals.title: Calibrating Parental Report: An Item Response Theory Analysis of the Global Impression Scales in Pediatric Infectious Disease Follow-up
proposals.motivation: Parental global impression scales are a cornerstone of assessing long-term outcomes in children after infections like COVID-19, PANS/PANDAS, or chronic rhinosinusitis, but their ordinal rating categories (e.g., 'much worse' to 'much better') are treated as equal-interval, reducing measurement precision. This psychometric limitation hinders accurate tracking of subtle developmental changes and complicates clinical research on post-infectious sequelae. Improved measurement is critical for creating reliable 'inventories' of patient-reported outcomes that clinicians and researchers can trust.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Item Response Theory (IRT) models to parental global ratings will reveal that the psychological distances between adjacent response categories (e.g., 'a little better' vs. 'much better') are not equal, and that calibrating these scales using IRT parameters will significantly improve the precision and sensitivity of measuring child health trajectories.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal datasets containing parental global impression ratings of child health and development from pediatric infectious disease and otolaryngology clinics. Using the R `mirt` package, we will fit polytomous IRT models (e.g., Graded Response Model) to the ordinal response data. The analysis will estimate item difficulty parameters (location of each rating threshold on the latent health trait) and discrimination parameters for each scale item, allowing us to construct a calibrated interval-level metric from the original ordinal scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, psychometrically robust method for converting common parental global ratings into precise continuous measures, enhancing the detection of small but clinically meaningful changes in child health. It will contribute a novel analytical framework for outcome measurement in post-acute infection syndromes, directly supporting more sensitive clinical research and inventory development as called for in recent proposals to improve research methodologies.
proposals.novelty: While IRT is standard in educational testing, its application to calibrate parental global impression scales in pediatric otolaryngology and infectious disease contexts is novel. This approach moves beyond treating these scales as simple summed scores, addressing a fundamental but overlooked measurement issue in clinical research reliant on parent report.

proposals.title: Generalizability of Cochlear Implant Timing-Outcome Relationships in Resource-Limited Settings: A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implantation (CI) outcomes in high-income countries are strongly tied to early diagnosis and timely intervention, particularly for pediatric patients. However, in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), delayed diagnosis due to limited screening, scarce clinicians, and financial barriers is common. It is unknown whether established prognostic models for CI success, developed in high-resource contexts, remain valid when applied to populations with prolonged auditory deprivation and constrained post-operative rehabilitation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between duration of deafness and post-implant speech perception outcomes will be significantly attenuated in LMIC settings, where extended delays and variable rehabilitation access introduce confounding socioeconomic and clinical factors not captured in existing models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective and prospective multi-center cohort study across CI programs in 3-4 LMICs. Primary outcomes (e.g., CAP/SIR scores) and key predictors (age at implantation, duration of deafness, rehabilitation frequency) will be collected via a standardized electronic inventory. We will employ multivariable linear and logistic regression models, adjusting for country, socioeconomic status, and caregiver (parent) involvement, to develop a revised outcome prediction algorithm. The analysis will specifically test for interaction effects between duration of deafness and a composite 'resource-access' variable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, context-specific prognostic tool for CI outcomes in LMICs, directly informing patient selection and counseling. It will challenge the direct export of high-income country clinical guidelines and highlight modifiable factors, such as parent-mediated therapy, that can optimize outcomes despite systemic delays.
proposals.novelty: While timing-outcome relationships are well-studied, their fundamental generalizability across healthcare ecosystems is untested. This proposal uniquely frames delayed diagnosis not merely as a covariate but as a central effect modifier, integrating health services research with traditional audiological outcome measurement.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Multi-institutional Comparison of Long-term Cosmetic, Functional, and Oncologic Outcomes for Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS) versus Transoral Laser Microsurgery (TLM) in Early Oropharyngeal Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: Transoral surgery for oropharyngeal cancer, primarily via TORS and TLM, offers organ preservation but with distinct technical approaches. Pre-2026, comparative studies have focused on short-term perioperative metrics and survival, leaving a critical evidence gap regarding long-term patient-centered outcomes. A comprehensive, prospective analysis of scar satisfaction, voice/swallow function, and disease recurrence is needed to inform surgical selection and counsel patients effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TORS will demonstrate superior long-term patient-reported scar satisfaction and comparable voice outcomes, while TLM will be associated with lower long-term local recurrence rates in early-stage (T1-T2) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
proposals.method: We propose a prospective, multi-institutional cohort study enrolling patients undergoing TORS or TLM for T1-T2 OPSCC. Standardized video-endoscopic exams and patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) will be collected pre-operatively and at 3, 6, 12, 24, and 36 months post-operatively. Primary outcomes: 1) Scar satisfaction via the Patient Scar Assessment Questionnaire (PSAQ), 2) Voice Handicap Index-10 (VHI-10) scores, and 3) local recurrence rates confirmed by biopsy and imaging. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects models to compare longitudinal outcomes between cohorts, controlling for covariates (e.g., HPV status, adjuvant therapy).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level, prospective evidence directly comparing long-term cosmetic and functional quality of life between the two dominant transoral approaches. It will generate a robust dataset to guide shared surgical decision-making and establish a benchmark for future technique refinements. The findings will be directly relevant to clinicians, patients, and health systems evaluating the value of these technologies.
proposals.novelty: While prior comparisons exist, this study's novelty lies in its prospective design with extended follow-up specifically targeting the understudied triad of cosmesis, voice, and recurrence concurrently, moving beyond short-term morbidity and survival endpoints dominant in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Prototypical Network for Few-Shot Diagnosis of Rare Otoscopic Pathologies
proposals.motivation: Diagnosis of rare ear pathologies, such as malignant otitis externa or rare congenital malformations, is challenging due to the scarcity of labeled training data. This leads to delayed diagnosis and suboptimal patient outcomes. Current deep learning models for otoscopic image analysis require large datasets, which are impractical for these rare conditions, creating a critical gap in automated diagnostic support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a metric-based few-shot learning framework can achieve clinically acceptable diagnostic accuracy for rare otologic conditions by learning a robust feature embedding from a base dataset of common pathologies, enabling generalization to novel rare classes with only a handful of examples.
proposals.method: We will employ a Prototypical Network architecture, a well-established metric-based meta-learning approach. The model will be pre-trained on a large base dataset of common otoscopic conditions (e.g., acute otitis media, cerumen impaction). In the few-shot stage, we will compute prototype vectors for each rare disease class from 1-5 support images per class. Query images will be classified by measuring Euclidean distance to these prototypes in the learned embedding space. We will use otoscopic video frames, leveraging Dr. Clark's recent work with video data, to create the support and query sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for data-efficient AI in otology, directly addressing the 'long-tail' problem of rare diseases. It will yield a novel diagnostic tool that can be adapted by clinicians as new rare conditions are encountered, potentially reducing diagnostic delays. The project will also create a structured inventory and benchmark dataset for rare ear pathologies to aid future research.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning has been explored in general medical imaging, its application to otoscopic diagnosis, specifically for constructing a dynamic diagnostic inventory for rare conditions, is novel. The integration of meta-learning principles with otoscopic video analysis, a domain where Dr. Clark has established expertise, represents a distinct methodological advance.

proposals.title: Automated, Fine-Grained Severity Grading of Otitis Media from Video Otoscopy: A Deep Learning Approach to Support Primary Care Diagnosis and Parental Counseling
proposals.motivation: Otitis media (OM) is a leading cause of pediatric primary care visits and antibiotic prescriptions. Current AI diagnostic tools focus on broad multi-class disease detection, which lacks the granularity needed for nuanced clinical management decisions. A severity grading system for OM, accessible to primary care clinicians, could standardize diagnosis, improve antibiotic stewardship, and provide a clear visual aid for explaining disease progression to parents.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep learning model trained on expert-labeled video otoscopy sequences can accurately grade the severity of otitis media (e.g., normal, mild effusion, bulging, severe bulging with erythema) with performance comparable to otolaryngologists, thereby providing a scalable decision-support tool.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset of pediatric ear exam videos from primary care and otolaryngology clinics, annotated frame-by-frame by clinicians using a standardized OM severity scale (e.g., a modified MEEi scale). A convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, such as a ResNet-50 or EfficientNet, pre-trained on ImageNet, will be adapted for video analysis using a temporal pooling layer or a lightweight 3D-CNN to aggregate frame-level features. The model will be trained for ordinal regression to predict severity grades, with performance validated against independent clinician ratings.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first AI tool specifically designed for fine-grained OM severity assessment from video, moving beyond binary detection. It will provide a validated, objective metric for tracking disease progression in clinical research and a practical visual tool to enhance clinician-parent communication during diagnosis and treatment planning.
proposals.novelty: Shifting the AI focus from multi-disease classification to granular severity grading within a single high-burden condition addresses a critical gap in actionable decision support. Leveraging video otoscopy, rather than static images, captures dynamic clinical features essential for accurate severity assessment, a modality underutilized in pre-2026 OM AI research.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning Model for Predicting Topical Treatment Response in Chronic Otitis Externa from Baseline Otoscopic Images
proposals.motivation: Chronic otitis externa (COE) is a common infection with variable treatment outcomes, leading to prolonged patient discomfort and repeated clinical visits. Current management relies on clinician experience, with no objective tool to predict which patients will respond to standard topical therapy versus those requiring escalation. This creates inefficiency and delays in care, particularly in resource-limited settings or during high-volume periods like the COVID-19 pandemic, where remote assessment tools are valuable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep learning models can extract prognostic visual biomarkers from baseline otoscopic images to accurately predict the likelihood of treatment failure with first-line topical antibiotics in COE.
proposals.method: We will curate a retrospective dataset of otoscopic images linked to documented treatment outcomes (success/failure after 2-week course). Using a pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture like ResNet-50 or EfficientNet as a feature extractor, we will train a binary classifier. Model development will involve data augmentation, transfer learning, and validation on a held-out test set. Performance will be evaluated using AUC-ROC, with model explanations generated via Grad-CAM to highlight image regions influencing predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated tool for prognostic stratification in COE based on a routine diagnostic image, moving beyond simple diagnosis. It will offer clinicians a data-driven aid for initial management decisions, potentially reducing treatment delays and optimizing antibiotic stewardship. The model could be integrated into telehealth platforms to support remote patient triage.
proposals.novelty: While prior AI work in otoscopy has focused on diagnostic classification (e.g., otitis media vs. externa), predicting treatment outcomes from a single baseline image is a novel, prognostically significant task. This shifts the AI application from a diagnostic 'inventory' tool to a clinical decision-support system for management planning.

proposals.title: The Diagnostic Utility of Odontalgia in Refractory Chronic Rhinosinusitis: A Prospective Blinded Cohort Study Quantifying the Prevalence and Accuracy of Pain for Detecting Odontogenic Sinusitis
proposals.motivation: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) refractory to medical therapy is a significant clinical burden. Odontogenic sinusitis (ODS) is a recognized but often underdiagnosed etiology, with pre-2026 literature suggesting it may account for 10-40% of unilateral cases, yet diagnosis is frequently delayed. Current reliance on patient-reported dental pain is problematic, as anecdotal evidence indicates many ODS cases are painless, leading to missed diagnoses, unnecessary antibiotic courses, and failed sinus surgeries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patient-reported odontalgia has low sensitivity but high specificity for detecting ODS in a refractory CRS cohort, and that the absence of dental pain cannot reliably exclude an odontogenic source.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study enrolling consecutive adult patients with CRS refractory to standard medical therapy. All participants will undergo a standardized dental pain assessment using a visual analog scale and a structured questionnaire. The reference standard will be cone-beam CT (CBCT) imaging of the sinuses and dentition. Two blinded, independent adjudicators (an otolaryngologist and an endodontist) will review all CBCTs and clinical data to determine the presence of ODS using established radiographic and clinical criteria. Diagnostic accuracy metrics (sensitivity, specificity, positive/negative predictive values) of odontalgia for ODS will be calculated, along with the overall prevalence of ODS in this cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first population-level, prospective data on the prevalence of ODS in a general refractory CRS population, moving beyond biased surgical case series. It will deliver actionable, quantified diagnostic accuracy metrics for the symptom of odontalgia, directly informing clinical decision-making. The results will establish an evidence-based threshold for when routine dental imaging (CBCT) is justified in the CRS diagnostic workup.
proposals.novelty: This study directly addresses the critical gap between anecdotal recognition of 'silent' ODS and the lack of quantitative data on its frequency and the diagnostic performance of its most common symptom. By employing a blinded, multi-specialty adjudication process in an unselected cohort, it overcomes the referral and confirmation biases inherent in prior retrospective studies.

proposals.title: Prevalence and Prognostic Impact of Occult Odontogenic Chronic Rhinosinusitis in Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Matched cohort studies in head and neck cancer (HNC) control for tumor site and stage but ignore the local tissue microenvironment. Chronic rhinosinusitis of odontogenic origin (CRS-O) is a common, often asymptomatic inflammatory condition that alters mucosal immunity and sinonasal flora. Its unrecognized presence could systematically bias analyses of treatment complications, recurrence, and survival, potentially confounding the assessment of established factors like HPV status.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that occult odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis is a prevalent, unmeasured confounder in HNC outcome studies and is independently associated with increased rates of postoperative complications and poorer disease-specific survival, even after adjusting for HPV status and standard matching variables.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 300 patients with newly diagnosed oropharyngeal and sinonasal squamous cell carcinoma. All participants will undergo standardized pre-treatment evaluation including a high-resolution sinonasal CT scan and a panoramic dental radiograph (orthopantomogram), interpreted by a blinded radiologist and dental specialist to diagnose CRS-O. Clinical data, HPV status (via p16 immunohistochemistry and/or PCR), and treatment details will be collected. Primary outcomes (post-operative infection/complication rates, 2-year disease-specific survival) will be analyzed using multivariable logistic and Cox proportional hazards regression, with CRS-O status as a key independent variable alongside HPV, T/N category, smoking, and comorbidities.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify the prevalence of a previously ignored biological confounder in HNC research. It will provide adjusted effect estimates for the impact of occult odontogenic disease on oncologic outcomes, offering a methodological framework for refining future matched studies. The findings could explain a portion of unexplained outcome variance in existing literature and inform pre-treatment screening protocols.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely fuses diagnostic paradigms from rhinology (CRS-O) with oncologic epidemiology, proposing a specific, image-detectable biological variable that is absent from all current HNC matching algorithms. It directly tests the confounder hypothesis rather than merely describing the disease's prevalence.

proposals.title: Population-Level Correlation of Subclinical Periapical Radiolucencies and Chronic Rhinosinusitis in the Geriatric Population: A Cone-Beam CT Screening Study
proposals.motivation: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is a major source of morbidity in the elderly, yet a significant proportion of cases remain idiopathic or poorly managed. While odontogenic CRS is recognized, current clinical focus is on overt oroantral fistulae, missing potential subclinical pathways. This study addresses the critical gap in understanding whether silent, micro-scale spread from periapical infections, prevalent in aging dentition, is a hidden driver of CRS burden at a population level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the prevalence of subclinical periapical radiolucencies adjacent to the maxillary sinus floor is significantly higher in elderly patients with a CRS diagnosis compared to those without, and that the severity of these lesions correlates with CRS symptom scores, independent of fistula formation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, population-level cohort study using existing cone-beam CT (CBCT) scans from a large dental imaging database of patients aged 65+. A deep learning algorithm (a 3D convolutional neural network, e.g., a 3D U-Net architecture) will be trained on a manually annotated dataset to automatically detect and segment periapical radiolucencies within 2mm of the maxillary sinus floor. The algorithm's output (prevalence, volume, location) will be linked via de-identified patient IDs to electronic health records to ascertain CRS diagnoses (ICD-10 codes) and Lund-Mackay CT scores from concurrent or subsequent ENT imaging. Statistical analysis will employ multivariate logistic regression, controlling for age, smoking, and diabetes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, quantitative evidence linking subclinical odontogenic pathology to CRS in the elderly. It will establish a population-attributable risk for this hidden mechanism, directly informing clinical practice. The findings could redefine screening paradigms, suggesting that incidental periapical findings on dental CBCT in elderly patients warrant formal ENT evaluation.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond case reports of fistulae and coarse dental-health surveys by using precise, automated 3D image analysis to test a specific subclinical pathomechanism at scale. It fuses large dental imaging datasets with ENT diagnostic data in a way not previously attempted to answer a long-suspected but unproven clinical question.

proposals.title: Prevalence and Radiographic Patterns of Multi-Sinus Involvement in Odontogenic Chronic Rhinosinusitis: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis (OCRS) is traditionally described as a disease isolated to the maxillary sinus. However, clinical experience and emerging case reports suggest potential extension to the frontal and ethmoid sinuses, which could alter diagnostic criteria and surgical management. This discrepancy between classic teaching and observed clinical patterns necessitates a systematic, evidence-based investigation to guide clinicians in accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of patients with confirmed OCRS will exhibit radiographic evidence of inflammatory disease extending beyond the maxillary sinus into the ipsilateral anterior ethmoid and/or frontal sinuses, challenging the paradigm of its localized nature.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients diagnosed with OCRS (confirmed by CT and dental pathology) from 2015-2025 at a tertiary care center. Using pre-2026 deep learning architectures (specifically, a pre-trained 3D convolutional neural network like 3D ResNet-50) adapted for medical image analysis, we will systematically quantify sinus opacification in all ipsilateral paranasal sinuses from preoperative CT scans. The algorithm will segment each sinus cavity (maxillary, anterior/posterior ethmoid, frontal, sphenoid) using a pre-existing atlas and calculate Lund-Mackay scores. Manual validation by two blinded rhinologists will ensure accuracy. Clinical and dental data will be correlated with radiographic findings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative analysis of sinus involvement patterns in OCRS, moving beyond anecdotal reports. It will establish evidence-based prevalence rates for multi-sinus disease, potentially leading to revised diagnostic criteria and more comprehensive treatment protocols that address all involved sinuses, thereby improving patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While case reports exist, no pre-2026 study has systematically applied quantitative image analysis to challenge the foundational assumption of maxillary-only disease in OCRS. The use of an automated segmentation and scoring algorithm provides an objective, reproducible measure of disease extension that is novel for this specific condition.

proposals.title: The Odontogenic CRS Iceberg: A Prospective, Multi-Center Study to Determine the True Prevalence of Dental Pathology in Chronic Rhinosinusitis Using Systematic Dual-Organ Screening
proposals.motivation: Current estimates suggest 10-30% of chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is odontogenic (OCRS), but these are based on retrospective data and symptom-triggered referrals, leading to significant detection bias. This mirrors the pre-screening era of celiac disease, where reliance on clinical presentation vastly underestimated true prevalence. Determining the accurate burden of OCRS is critical for effective treatment, as failure to address a dental source leads to persistent infection and surgical failure.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic, protocol-driven screening for dental pathology in all CRS patients will reveal a true prevalence of odontogenic contribution significantly higher than current estimates, demonstrating an 'iceberg' phenomenon where most cases are clinically occult.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center cohort study enrolling consecutive adult CRS patients. All participants will undergo standardized dual-modality screening: 1) Cone-beam CT (CBCT) of the maxillary dentition, analyzed using predefined radiographic criteria (e.g., periapical lucency >3mm, oroantral communication), and 2) Comprehensive periodontal examination by a calibrated dentist, assessing pocket depth and mobility of posterior maxillary teeth. Prevalence will be calculated as the proportion of patients meeting predefined diagnostic criteria for OCRS. Multi-variable regression will analyze factors (e.g., geography, prior dental care) associated with screening-detected vs. previously known OCRS.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first unbiased, population-based estimate of OCRS prevalence within a CRS cohort, establishing a new epidemiological benchmark. It will create a validated screening protocol for clinical use and inform guidelines on when to integrate dental evaluation into routine rhinology practice, potentially improving treatment outcomes.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the successful 'iceberg model' epidemiological approach from celiac disease seroprevalence studies to a surgical-dental comorbidity, moving beyond retrospective, biased designs to active, prospective screening of all patients in a defined clinical pathway.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Multi-Center Study to Determine the True Prevalence of Odontogenic Chronic Rhinosinusitis (OCRS) Through Systematic Dental Screening
proposals.motivation: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is a major public health burden, and odontogenic origin (OCRS) is a significant but poorly quantified subtype. Current prevalence estimates of 10-30% are based on retrospective data and likely underrepresent the true incidence due to inconsistent diagnostic pathways. A precise prevalence figure is critical for guiding cost-effective diagnostic algorithms, improving surgical outcomes, and reducing unnecessary antibiotic use by identifying and treating the dental source.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the true prevalence of odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis (OCRS) in a general CRS patient population is greater than 20% and that systematic dental examination will identify a significant proportion of cases missed by standard otolaryngologic evaluation alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center cohort study enrolling consecutive adult patients presenting with CRS. All participants will undergo a standardized clinical protocol: 1) a structured ENT history and nasal endoscopy, 2) sinus CT scan reviewed with a validated radiographic scoring system (e.g., Lund-Mackay), and 3) a systematic dental examination including panoramic radiography (OPG) and cone-beam CT (CBCT) for suspicious findings, performed by a calibrated dentist blinded to ENT findings. Diagnosis of definitive OCRS will require fulfillment of both radiographic (e.g., periapical lucency, oroantral fistula) and clinical criteria. Data will be analyzed using descriptive statistics and logistic regression to identify predictive factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, prospective estimate of OCRS prevalence in a multi-center setting, establishing a definitive benchmark for the field. It will yield a validated clinical and radiographic protocol for diagnosing OCRS, directly informing future clinical practice guidelines. Furthermore, it will clarify the demographic and clinical characteristics of OCRS patients, aiding in targeted diagnosis.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies are retrospective and often single-center, this is a prospective, protocol-driven study that mandates systematic dental imaging for all CRS patients, eliminating the selection bias inherent in prior work. The integration of blinded, standardized dental evaluation with CBCT into the routine CRS workup represents a novel, comprehensive diagnostic approach.

proposals.title: Prospective Validation of an Integrated ENT-Dental Diagnostic Pathway: A Multicenter Trial to Determine Prevalence and Diagnostic Accuracy for Odontogenic Chronic Rhinosinusitis
proposals.motivation: Odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis (OCRS) is a significant but often underdiagnosed subtype of CRS, leading to prolonged patient suffering, inappropriate antibiotic use, and failed sinus surgeries when the dental source is missed. Current diagnostic pathways are based on retrospective data and expert opinion, lacking prospective validation. This creates a critical evidence gap for clinicians in otolaryngology and dentistry, hindering the development of efficient, cost-effective referral protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: A standardized, prospective ENT-dental diagnostic pathway incorporating systematic dental examination and cone-beam CT (CBCT) will accurately identify OCRS and reveal its true prevalence is higher than historically reported, particularly in patients with unilateral symptoms or antibiotic-refractory disease.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter prospective cohort trial. All consenting adult patients meeting diagnostic criteria for CRS will undergo the integrated pathway: standardized ENT evaluation, systematic dental examination by a study dentist, and dedicated dental CBCT. The reference standard for OCRS will be a composite of clinical, radiographic, and surgical findings. We will calculate the pathway's diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) and the point prevalence of OCRS, stratified by laterality, antibiotic response, and dental risk factors. An adaptive design will allow for mid-trial refinement of screening triggers based on interim prevalence analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, multicenter data on the prevalence and diagnostic features of OCRS. It will yield a validated, evidence-based clinical pathway that specifies which CRS patients require mandatory dental evaluation, directly informing future clinical guidelines. The results aim to reduce diagnostic delay, optimize resource use, and improve patient outcomes by ensuring accurate etiologic diagnosis.
proposals.novelty: This work fuses a robust prevalence study within a diagnostic validation trial, creating a feedback loop where epidemiological data directly calibrates the clinical pathway in real-time. This approach moves beyond retrospective case series to generate prospectively derived, evidence-calibrated screening recommendations.

proposals.title: A Prospective Staged-Escalation Protocol for Odontogenic Sinusitis: Identifying Predictors for Endoscopic Sinus Surgery After Dental Treatment Failure
proposals.motivation: Odontogenic sinusitis (ODS) is a distinct, often refractory form of chronic rhinosinusitis, yet optimal management remains unclear. Retrospective studies comparing initial dental treatment alone versus combined dental and sinus surgery are confounded by selection bias, as patients with more severe disease are often selected for combined procedures. This creates significant uncertainty for clinicians and patients regarding the true incremental benefit of endoscopic sinus surgery (ESS) when definitive dental treatment is performed first.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of ODS patients will achieve resolution with dental treatment alone, and that baseline clinical and radiographic features can predict which patients will ultimately require surgical escalation.
proposals.method: This is a prospective, single-arm cohort study. Consecutive patients with confirmed ODS will be enrolled into a standardized staged protocol: all undergo definitive dental treatment (extraction/endodontic therapy). At 3 months, patients are reassessed using SNOT-22, Lund-Mackay scoring on cone-beam CT, and endoscopic findings. Pre-registered failure criteria (e.g., SNOT-22 improvement <9 points, persistent opacification) trigger escalation to ESS. The primary outcome is the proportion requiring surgery. Secondary analysis employs multivariable logistic regression to build a prediction model using baseline variables: duration of dental pathology, Lund-Mackay score, presence of fungal elements on culture, and size of oroantral communication.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, unbiased estimate of the true failure rate of dental treatment for ODS, directly quantifying the added value of ESS. It will generate a clinically actionable prediction model to guide personalized treatment decisions, potentially sparing low-risk patients from unnecessary surgery while identifying high-risk patients for early combined intervention.
proposals.novelty: The within-cohort, staged-escalation design inherently controls for confounding by indication, a major limitation of all prior comparative studies. This approach prospectively creates the necessary counterfactual to isolate the effect of sinus surgery following adequate dental source control.

proposals.title: A Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial (SMART) to Optimize Treatment Sequencing in Odontogenic Chronic Rhinosinusitis
proposals.motivation: Odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis (OCRS) is a common and debilitating infection, yet optimal treatment sequencing remains unclear. Current evidence, largely retrospective, conflates selection bias with efficacy, failing to determine whether combined dental and sinus surgery upfront is superior to a sequential approach starting with dental treatment alone. This gap leads to inconsistent clinical practice, potential overtreatment with surgery, and unresolved disease for some patients.
proposals.hypothesis: A sequential treatment strategy, where only patients who fail to respond to initial dental intervention proceed to combined dental-sinus surgery, will yield equivalent long-term sinusitis resolution rates to an upfront combined strategy, while reducing overall surgical burden and healthcare costs.
proposals.method: We will implement a SMART design. All enrolled patients with confirmed OCRS will first undergo standardized dental treatment (extraction/endodontic therapy). At 12 weeks, response is assessed via SNOT-22, endoscopy, and CT Lund-Mackay score. Non-responders are then re-randomized to either endoscopic sinus surgery (combined intervention) or continued medical management. Prespecified tailoring variables (e.g., ostiomeatal complex patency, culture profiles) will be analyzed to develop personalized decision rules. Primary endpoints are complete resolution at 12 and 24 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a dynamic, response-adaptive treatment algorithm in OCRS. It will generate a data-driven clinical decision rule to personalize therapy, directly informing practice guidelines. Furthermore, it will demonstrate the feasibility and value of transplanting adaptive trial designs from oncology into infectious and inflammatory disease research.
proposals.novelty: This is the first application of the SMART framework, a sophisticated oncology trial methodology, to the management of a focal sinus infection. Its novelty lies in dynamically testing treatment sequences rather than static comparisons, explicitly modeling the clinical decision point faced after initial dental therapy fails.

proposals.title: A CBCT-Based Radiomic Model to Predict Surgical Necessity in Odontogenic Sinusitis: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Odontogenic sinusitis (ODS) is a distinct, often recalcitrant form of chronic rhinosinusitis, but treatment pathways remain ambiguous. While some patients resolve with dental treatment alone, others require combined dental and endoscopic sinus surgery (ESS), leading to potential overtreatment or delayed intervention. Pre-operative cone-beam CT (CBCT) scans, routinely obtained for dental implant or extraction planning, are underutilized for predicting sinus disease trajectory, representing a missed opportunity for personalized, cost-effective care.
proposals.hypothesis: Quantitative radiomic features extracted from pre-operative dental CBCT scans—specifically volumetric mucosal thickening, ostiomeatal complex obstruction, and the extent of dental pathology breaching the sinus floor—can stratify ODS patients into those with a high likelihood of resolution after dental treatment alone versus those who will require ESS for definitive management.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients with confirmed ODS who underwent dental CBCT prior to treatment. Using 3D Slicer and ITK-SNAP, we will segment the maxillary sinus and quantify mucosal thickening volume. A convolutional neural network (CNN) based on a U-Net architecture will be trained to automatically assess ostiomeatal complex patency. These imaging biomarkers, along with clinical variables, will be used to train a logistic regression and a random forest classifier to predict the binary outcome of sinusitis resolution without ESS at 6-month follow-up, using a held-out test set for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated clinical decision support tool that leverages existing dental CBCT data to guide ODS treatment stratification. It directly addresses the clinical dilemma of surgical timing, aiming to reduce unnecessary ESS in low-risk patients while ensuring prompt referral for high-risk cases. The study will establish specific, quantifiable CBCT criteria for surgical necessity in ODS.
proposals.novelty: While CBCT is standard in dental diagnostics and outcome studies for ODS exist, no pre-2026 work has fused volumetric image analysis with machine learning to create a predictive model from this ubiquitous, low-dose imaging modality specifically to guide the dental-versus-combined-surgery decision.

proposals.title: Estimating the Causal Effect of Dental Health Deterioration on Chronic Rhinosinusitis Incidence in Elderly Adults Using Instrumental Variable and Negative Control Designs
proposals.motivation: Odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is increasingly recognized as a distinct clinical entity, yet its true burden in elderly populations remains unknown because observational studies cannot disentangle dental pathology from shared geriatric confounders such as polypharmacy, immunosenescence, and mucosal atrophy. Prior work, including Clark and colleagues' characterization of odontogenic CRS phenotypes, has established biological plausibility but relied on cross-sectional or retrospective designs vulnerable to unmeasured confounding. A rigorous causal inference framework is needed to determine whether preventive dental care could meaningfully reduce CRS incidence in aging populations and to guide evidence-based screening policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Dental health deterioration, as measured by progressive periodontal disease severity and periapical pathology, causally increases the incidence of maxillary-predominant CRS in adults aged 65 and older, with an effect that persists after accounting for shared systemic inflammatory confounders through instrumental variable and negative control outcome analyses.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site prospective cohort study enrolling approximately 2,400 adults aged 65 or older from six geographically diverse U.S. health systems, with baseline and 12-month standardized periodontal examinations, periapical radiographs, and cone-beam CT imaging. Regional variation in dental insurance generosity (Medicare Advantage dental benefit tiers) and community water fluoridation status will serve as instrumental variables for dental health exposure, estimated via two-stage least squares and control-function approaches with weak-instrument diagnostics including the Kleibergen-Paap F-statistic. CRS outcomes will be adjudicated using EPOS 2020 criteria with confirmatory CT Lund-Mackay scoring.
