Negative control outcomes—sensorineural hearing loss and osteoarthritis flares—that share geriatric confounders but lack a plausible odontogenic pathway will be analyzed in parallel; any observed instrument-outcome association for these controls will quantify residual bias using the framework of Lipsitch, Tchetgen Tchetgen, and Cohen (2010). Mediation analysis using natural effect models will decompose the total effect into direct contiguous-spread and indirect systemic inflammatory pathways, with serum CRP and IL-6 as candidate mediators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causally interpretable estimate of the population-attributable fraction of CRS due to dental pathology in the elderly, directly informing whether routine dental screening should be incorporated into geriatric preventive care guidelines. The negative control validation will establish a reusable bias-calibration template for future otolaryngologic epidemiology in comorbid populations. Additionally, the mediation decomposition will clarify whether systemic inflammation or direct anatomic spread is the predominant mechanism, guiding targeted intervention design.
proposals.novelty: No prior study of odontogenic CRS has employed instrumental variables or negative control outcomes to address confounding; applying these econometric and epidemiologic tools to a rhinologic question in geriatric populations is methodologically unprecedented and transforms an inherently confounded clinical association into an actionable causal estimate.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Risk-Stratifying Odontogenic Chronic Rhinosinusitis in Geriatric Patients Using Integrated Dental and Comorbidity Data
proposals.motivation: Chronic rhinosinusitis of odontogenic origin (OD-CRS) is a significant source of morbidity in the elderly, a population with high rates of dental pathology and under-recognized sinus disease. Current diagnosis relies on symptom-driven imaging, missing opportunities for early intervention. There is a critical need for proactive, data-driven screening tools that leverage routine dental health data to identify high-risk individuals before symptomatic progression.
proposals.hypothesis: Supervised machine learning models, trained on linked geriatric dental and medical records, can identify a parsimonious set of oral-health and demographic features that accurately predict the risk of developing clinically significant OD-CRS, enabling effective risk stratification.
proposals.method: We will construct a retrospective cohort from linked electronic health record and dental claim databases, defining OD-CRS cases via ICD/CPT codes and CT confirmation. Feature engineering will extract variables from dental records (e.g., periodontal indices, periapical lesions, edentulism), demographics, and comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, immunosuppression). We will adapt predictive pipelines from oncology informatics, specifically employing random forest and gradient boosting (XGBoost) for classification, and Cox proportional hazards models for time-to-event analysis, to build and validate risk-stratification models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated clinical decision-support algorithm for OD-CRS risk prediction in the elderly, directly translating routine dental exam findings into actionable ENT referral guidance. It will establish a novel application of surgical oncology triage methodologies to the dental-sinus disease axis, creating a scalable model for data-driven prevention in otolaryngology.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of dental epidemiology with machine learning techniques proven in surgical oncology (like TORS patient selection) for OD-CRS prediction is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive association studies to create a deployable tool for pre-symptomatic risk assessment, a capability absent in both population dentistry and current ENT practice.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Clinical Prediction Rule for Odontogenic Sinusitis in Children: A Multicenter Diagnostic Study
proposals.motivation: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) of odontogenic origin is a well-established entity in adults but is systematically underdiagnosed in children, leading to prolonged symptoms, inappropriate antibiotic use, and failed medical therapy. This diagnostic gap mirrors the historical challenge of pediatric UTIs, where low clinical suspicion and nonspecific symptoms delayed care. Establishing evidence-based screening guidelines for pediatric odontogenic sinusitis is crucial to improve diagnostic accuracy and patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinical prediction rule, incorporating specific dental and radiographic findings from deciduous or mixed dentition, can accurately identify children with chronic sinusitis who have an underlying odontogenic source, thereby enabling targeted diagnosis and treatment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multicenter diagnostic study enrolling children aged 4-12 with chronic/recurrent sinusitis unresponsive to standard therapy. For each participant, clinicians will systematically collect a standardized inventory of clinical predictors (e.g., deep caries in specific deciduous molars, focal tenderness). All participants will undergo limited-field cone-beam CT (CBCT) as the reference standard. We will use multivariable logistic regression to identify independent predictors of odontogenic sinusitis and derive a weighted clinical prediction rule, which will be internally validated using bootstrapping techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prevalence estimate of odontogenic sinusitis in a pediatric CRS cohort and deliver a validated, practical screening instrument for clinicians. It will establish a new diagnostic paradigm in pediatric ENT and dental practice, shifting odontogenic sinusitis from an overlooked etiology to a routinely considered differential, similar to the impact of UTI screening protocols.
proposals.novelty: This study innovatively applies the diagnostic framework successfully used for pediatric UTIs—lowering testing thresholds and creating age-specific rules—to the novel context of pediatric sinusitis, a direct analogy not previously explored. It focuses specifically on the understudied role of deciduous teeth pathology, moving beyond adult-centric models of odontogenic infection.

proposals.title: A Lifespan Cohort Analysis of Odontogenic Sinusitis: Linking Pediatric Dental Infections to Geriatric Sinus Disease Risk Using Population Health Registries
proposals.motivation: Odontogenic sinusitis (ODS) is a significant contributor to chronic rhinosinusitis, yet its natural history across the lifespan is poorly understood. Pre-2026 research, including Clark's work on ODS, is fragmented into pediatric or adult studies, missing critical longitudinal connections. This gap prevents the identification of early-life risk factors and intervention points that could reduce the substantial disease burden and healthcare costs associated with chronic sinusitis in aging populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Childhood odontogenic sinus infections, particularly from deciduous teeth, cause persistent alterations in maxillary sinus anatomy and local mucosal immunity that predispose individuals to recurrent or chronic rhinosinusitis in later adulthood and geriatric age.
proposals.method: We will establish a retrospective, population-level cohort by linking state-level dental treatment records, otolaryngology diagnoses (ICD-10 codes), and maxillofacial imaging archives from a single-payer health system. Individuals will be stratified into birth cohorts and tracked from childhood (<12 years) through older age (>65 years). Using time-to-event analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) and generalized estimating equations, we will model ODS incidence, with key predictors being documented childhood dental abscesses, periapical pathology on pediatric radiographs, and transitional dental states (mixed dentition, edentulism).
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first age-stratified risk model for ODS across the human lifespan, quantifying the long-term sinus disease risk attributable to early dental pathology. It will provide evidence-based timelines for preventive dental care and establish a framework for integrated dental-ENT surveillance in high-risk patients, directly informing clinical guidelines.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond cross-sectional or age-limited studies by fusing pediatric and geriatric epidemiology within a single longitudinal framework, explicitly testing the decades-later sequelae of early childhood dental infections on sinus health—a connection previously speculated but never empirically demonstrated at a population level.

proposals.title: Calibrating Patient-Reported Swallowing Outcomes to Instrumental Metrics After TORS: A Multivariate Ecological Calibration Model
proposals.motivation: Following Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS) for oropharyngeal cancer, patient-reported outcome (PRO) measures like the MD Anderson Dysphagia Inventory (MDADI) are crucial for assessing swallowing quality of life, yet they often correlate poorly with objective instrumental findings from videofluoroscopy. This discordance, analogous to discrepancies between observer reports and sensor data in ecological surveys, undermines clinical decision-making and the validity of outcome studies. A systematic method to calibrate subjective PROs against objective physiological benchmarks is needed to improve the interpretation of swallowing recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic biases exist between patient-reported swallowing function and instrumental metrics, and these biases can be modeled and corrected using a multivariate calibration framework adapted from ecological survey methods, with the discordance varying predictably by functional domain (e.g., liquids vs. solids) and patient phenotype (e.g., tumor location, receipt of adjuvant therapy).
proposals.method: We will prospectively collect paired PRO (MDADI, EAT-10) and instrumental (videofluoroscopic Penetration-Aspiration Scale, pharyngeal constriction ratio, residue scores) data from a cohort of TORS patients pre- and post-operatively. Adapting integrated models from ecology, we will employ a Bayesian multivariate regression framework to model the relationship between PRO scores and instrumental metrics. This model will include patient phenotype variables as covariates to estimate domain- and subgroup-specific calibration coefficients, effectively creating an algorithm to adjust raw PRO scores towards their instrumentally-predicted values.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated calibration algorithm for swallowing PROs in head and neck oncology, directly enhancing the clinical utility of PRO data by linking it to physiological function. It will provide a novel framework for outcome assessment that reconciles patient experience with objective measures, improving the precision of clinical trials and personalized post-TORS rehabilitation planning.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in head and neck oncology for its direct adaptation of ecological calibration models to solve a core measurement problem, moving beyond simple correlation to explicit bias correction. It integrates diverse data types (PRO, instrumental, clinical phenotype) in a single analytical framework specifically designed to handle systematic observer (patient) error.

proposals.title: A Prospective Cohort Study of De-escalated Adjuvant Therapy Guided by Geriatric Dental Health Risk Stratification in HPV-Associated Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 era, treatment de-escalation for HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (OPSCC) is a major research focus to reduce toxicity, but current strategies often rely on clinical staging and smoking history, which may not fully capture the biological resilience of elderly patients. Geriatric patients (>70 years) are particularly vulnerable to the severe toxicities of standard adjuvant chemoradiation, yet they are frequently excluded from de-escalation trials. Chronic dental infections and oral inflammatory conditions, such as odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis, represent a significant, measurable source of immunosenescence and systemic inflammation that could predict tolerance to therapy and oncologic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Elderly patients with HPV-associated OPSCC and superior baseline dental/periodontal health (low inflammatory burden) constitute a biologically favorable subgroup in whom de-escalated adjuvant radiotherapy alone will yield equivalent locoregional control and survival to standard adjuvant chemoradiation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-institutional cohort study of patients >70 years with HPV+ OPSCC treated with primary Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS). All patients will undergo a standardized pre-treatment dental health inventory, including panoramic radiography, periodontal charting, and screening for odontogenic CRS via sinus CT, to generate a composite Dental Inflammatory Burden Score (DIBS). Based on DIBS stratification, patients with low burden will be offered de-escalated adjuvant therapy (e.g., 50 Gy radiotherapy alone), while those with high burden will receive standard-of-care. Primary outcomes will be 2-year locoregional control and toxicity profiles, analyzed using Kaplan-Meier and Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective evidence for integrating geriatric-specific biological risk modifiers, derived from routine dental screening, into adjuvant therapy decision-making. It will establish a novel, practical risk-stratification framework to personalize de-escalation candidacy in a vulnerable, understudied population, potentially setting a new standard for pre-treatment assessment in elderly OPSCC.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond anatomic and clinical factors by directly incorporating a quantifiable, pre-existing oral health and chronic infection profile—a domain well-aligned with Dr. Clark's expertise—as a biological filter to identify elderly patients most likely to benefit from toxicity-sparing regimens, a concept not yet tested in prospective trials.

proposals.title: Overcoming Survivor Bias in Dysphagia Outcomes: A Hybrid Remote-Clinic Surveillance System with Competing-Risk Analysis for Longitudinal Swallowing Function
proposals.motivation: Long-term dysphagia outcomes after stroke, head/neck cancer, or neurodegenerative disease are poorly characterized due to high attrition in longitudinal studies, where the sickest patients are lost to follow-up, creating biased estimates of recovery. This survivor bias, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic's shift towards remote care, obscures the true incidence of late-onset dysphagia and its severe sequelae like aspiration pneumonia. A methodologically robust surveillance system is needed to capture the full spectrum of outcomes, including mortality and institutionalization, to inform clinical prognostication and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid monitoring system combining periodic in-clinic instrumental assessments with continuous remote patient-reported outcomes, analyzed using joint longitudinal-survival models and inverse-probability-of-censoring weighting, will yield unbiased estimates of swallowing function trajectories and accurately characterize the competing risks of mortality and major morbidity over a multi-year period.
proposals.method: We will recruit a prospective cohort of 300 patients with dysphagia from diverse etiologies. Data collection includes quarterly in-clinic videofluoroscopic swallowing studies (VFSS) and fiberoptic endoscopic evaluations of swallowing (FEES), supplemented by monthly automated digital patient-reported outcome (PRO) surveys via a secure platform. For cognitively impaired participants, caregiver-proxy surveys will be collected. Outcomes will be linked in real-time to national death registries and hospital discharge databases to track competing events (mortality, aspiration pneumonia admission, feeding tube placement). Statistical analysis will employ a joint model combining a linear mixed-effects model for the longitudinal swallowing scores (from VFSS/FEES/PROs) with a cause-specific Cox proportional hazards model for the time-to-event data, using inverse-probability-of-censoring weighting to adjust for informative dropout.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unbiased, longitudinal characterization of swallowing function trajectories and competing risks in a dysphagia population, directly addressing the critical methodological flaw of survivor bias. It will establish a validated, scalable framework for attrition-robust outcome surveillance that can be adapted to other chronic conditions. The results will deliver clinically actionable data on late-onset dysphagia incidence and long-term recovery patterns to improve patient counseling and preventive care pathways.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of competing-risk survival analysis with hybrid longitudinal monitoring (blending gold-standard clinic assessments with high-frequency remote PROs) to directly model and correct for attrition mechanisms linked to dysphagia severity, a methodological advance beyond traditional complete-case or last-observation-carried-forward analyses used in pre-2026 otolaryngology and speech-language pathology research.

proposals.title: Diverging Long-Term Trajectories: A Comparative Cohort Study of Source-Elimination vs. Symptom-Management in Odontogenic CRS and Late-Onset Dysphagia
proposals.motivation: In chronic head-and-neck conditions like odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) and post-treatment dysphagia, clinical trials and standard practice often rely on short-term (<12 month) outcomes, which may show initial equivalence between medical management and definitive source-elimination surgery. This risks underestimating long-term treatment failure, leading to patient morbidity, repeated interventions, and increased healthcare costs. A pre-2026 evidence gap exists in directly comparing long-term functional trajectories across different chronic conditions to model the universal importance of addressing root pathological sources.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with odontogenic CRS or conditions predisposing to late-onset dysphagia, interventions that fail to address the primary anatomical or infectious source will demonstrate outcome trajectories that significantly diverge from source-elimination treatments after 12 months, despite showing equivalent short-term success.
proposals.method: We will conduct a dual-domain, longitudinal observational cohort study. For odontogenic CRS, we will recruit patients treated either with extended antibiotics alone or with dental source surgery (e.g., tooth extraction, endodontic therapy). For dysphagia, we will recruit patients with head-and-neck tissue damage (e.g., from radiation or intubation) managed with supportive therapy alone versus those receiving early targeted intervention (e.g., dilation, injection). Primary outcomes are disease-specific quality-of-life (SNOT-22 for CRS, SWAL-QOL for dysphagia) and objective measures (endoscopic grading, videofluoroscopic swallowing study scores). Data will be collected at baseline, 3, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months. Trajectory analysis will use linear mixed-effects models with group-by-time interaction to test for divergence, and latent class growth analysis will identify subgroups with distinct long-term patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative, longitudinal evidence across two head-and-neck domains that source-elimination is critical for sustained recovery. It will generate a model of 'delayed treatment failure' applicable to other chronic conditions. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines, advocating for longer mandatory follow-up in outcome studies and a paradigm shift towards source-directed therapy.
proposals.novelty: The cross-domain design using dysphagia and CRS as parallel models for persistent source effects is novel, moving beyond single-disease studies. The explicit focus on trajectory divergence after 12 months challenges the sufficiency of short-term endpoints that currently dominate clinical research and insurance coverage decisions in otolaryngology.

proposals.title: Fusion of Wearable Swallowing Accelerometry and AI-Derived Anatomic Risk from Routine CT for Proactive Dysphagia Stratification
proposals.motivation: Dysphagia, a common sequela of head and neck disease, surgery, and infection, leads to aspiration pneumonia, malnutrition, and reduced quality of life. Current monitoring is episodic, relying on clinician-performed videofluoroscopy, which fails to capture daily functional fluctuations. There is a critical need for continuous, personalized monitoring that integrates a patient's unique, static anatomic risk with their dynamic swallowing function to predict clinical decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model fusing continuous swallowing biomechanics from a wearable accelerometer with quantified anatomic risk factors automatically extracted from routine head and neck CT scans will enable accurate, patient-specific prediction of functional swallowing decline and aspiration risk.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D U-Net architecture to automatically segment key pharyngeal and laryngeal structures from existing patient CT scans (e.g., dental, sinus, post-op) and compute metrics like pharyngeal lumen volume and hyoid position. Concurrently, we will process tri-axial accelerometry signals from a neck-worn sensor using time-frequency analysis and feature extraction. A multimodal fusion network (e.g., a late-fusion convolutional neural network) will integrate the anatomic and time-series features, trained on labeled outcomes of functional decline or aspiration events from videofluoroscopic swallow studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first closed-loop system for continuous dysphagia risk stratification by contextualizing real-time biomechanics within patient-specific anatomy. It will provide clinicians with a proactive tool for intervention planning, moving beyond simple tracking to predictive analytics. The method leverages pre-existing imaging, creating a low-burden, scalable monitoring paradigm for chronic conditions like post-surgical recovery or post-COVID dysphagia.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the AI-mediated fusion of a ubiquitous static data source (routine CT) with continuous wearable sensing for functional prediction, creating a personalized anatomic baseline against which daily swallowing is compared, a concept not explored in prior wearable or imaging-only dysphagia research.

proposals.title: Defining Volume-Outcome Benchmarks in Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS): A Multicenter Prospective Study of Instrumental Swallowing Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The volume-outcome relationship in Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS) for oropharyngeal cancer is well-established for oncologic endpoints but poorly defined for functional recovery, particularly swallowing. Current evidence relies heavily on subjective patient-reported outcomes, lacking the high-fidelity, objective physiological data needed to inform credentialing, center-of-excellence designations, and surgical training. This gap is critical as healthcare systems and payers increasingly demand objective benchmarks for quality and value-based care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable thresholds of institutional and surgeon TORS volume are directly correlated with superior, instrumentally-verified swallowing outcomes, and that these thresholds can be empirically defined to establish functional benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-institutional cohort study enrolling patients undergoing TORS for primary oropharyngeal carcinoma. A core protocol mandates standardized instrumental swallowing assessment (FEES or videofluoroscopy) at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months post-operatively, using a centralized, blinded analysis of validated metrics like the Penetration-Aspiration Scale and pharyngeal residue scores. Institutions will be stratified by annual TORS volume (low, medium, high). Statistical analysis will employ multivariable mixed-effects regression modeling to correlate volume with outcome trajectories, with receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis used to identify critical volume thresholds predictive of optimal functional recovery.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first objective, physiology-based evidence linking surgical volume to functional outcomes in TORS, moving beyond surrogate markers. It will generate data-driven volume thresholds that can directly inform healthcare policy, hospital credentialing, and the definition of Centers of Excellence. The harmonized dataset will also serve as a benchmark for future interventional studies aimed at improving swallowing rehabilitation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in mandating uniform, objective instrumental swallowing assessment across centers to transform 'volume' from a crude administrative proxy into a precise, physiologically-grounded predictor, creating a new model for defining surgical quality and competence in head and neck oncology.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Standardized Functional Assessment Protocol for Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS) in Laryngeal and Hypopharyngeal Cancers
proposals.motivation: Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS) is well-established for oropharyngeal cancers, with validated functional outcome measures. However, its application to laryngeal and hypopharyngeal subsites is expanding without a standardized, site-specific methodology to assess post-operative swallowing, voice, and airway outcomes. This gap hinders comparative effectiveness research, patient counseling, and the optimization of surgical techniques for these anatomically and functionally distinct regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, multi-modal assessment protocol, adapted from established TORS functional metrics and incorporating subsite-specific parameters, will provide a valid, reliable, and clinically meaningful measure of functional outcomes for laryngeal and hypopharyngeal TORS.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of patients undergoing TORS for laryngeal/hypopharyngeal cancers. The protocol will integrate: 1) Patient-reported outcomes (MD Anderson Dysphagia Inventory, Voice Handicap Index-10), 2) Objective clinician-rated scales (Penetration-Aspiration Scale, Functional Oral Intake Scale) assessed from standardized post-operative videofluoroscopic swallow studies, and 3) Peri-operative data from a surgical inventory documenting tumor and resection characteristics. Statistical analysis will employ correlation matrices and multivariate regression to validate the composite protocol against key endpoints like gastrostomy-tube dependence and aspiration pneumonia.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, comprehensive functional assessment toolkit for TORS beyond the oropharynx. It will establish a benchmark for functional reporting in future clinical trials and enable data-driven comparisons with non-surgical organ-preservation strategies, such as radiation-based protocols, for these cancers.
proposals.novelty: While TORS functional assessment exists for the oropharynx, this protocol is novel in its deliberate adaptation and integration of validated instruments to the unique functional priorities and anatomic challenges of the larynx and hypopharynx, creating a new disease- and subsite-specific standard.

proposals.title: Defining a Protective Core Ear Canal Microbiome: An Inverse Modeling Approach to Prevent Aspergillus Colonization in Chronic Otitis Externa
proposals.motivation: Chronic otitis externa, particularly fungal infections like those caused by Aspergillus species, is a persistent clinical challenge, often requiring prolonged antifungal therapy with variable success. Pre-2026 research has primarily focused on characterizing the dysbiotic microbiome during active infection. However, a proactive, preventive strategy based on defining a stable, protective microbial community is lacking. This study inverts the classic pathogenesis question to identify which resident bacterial consortia actively resist fungal colonization, offering a potential paradigm shift from treatment to prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a defined, stable core microbiome exists in the healthy ear canal, characterized by specific bacterial taxa and functional gene profiles, which confers colonization resistance against Aspergillus species through niche competition and metabolic inhibition.
proposals.method: We will conduct a case-control study comparing the ear canal microbiomes of healthy controls (n=50) and patients with culture-confirmed Aspergillus otitis externa (n=50) using 16S rRNA and ITS2 sequencing. Microbial community data will be analyzed using inverse statistical and network modeling techniques. Specifically, we will employ sparse inverse covariance estimation (e.g., Graphical Lasso) to reconstruct microbial interaction networks and use logistic regression with LASSO regularization to identify a minimal predictive set of protective bacterial taxa. These models will be validated in a separate cohort using qPCR for key taxa.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven definition of a 'protective' ear canal microbiome, moving beyond descriptive dysbiosis. It will deliver a specific, testable microbial signature associated with Aspergillus resistance. The findings could directly inform the development of next-generation probiotic or prebiotic interventions aimed at restoring or maintaining this protective state, reducing the incidence of refractory fungal otitis.
proposals.novelty: This proposal is novel in its application of an inverse problem framework—seeking the microbial state that prevents disease rather than the one that causes it—to otologic infection. It leverages advanced network and regularized regression models, common in other fields but not yet applied to define protective aural microbiomes, to move from correlation to prediction of colonization resistance.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning Video Otoscopy Screening Tool for the Early Detection of Subclinical Fungal Otitis Externa
proposals.motivation: Invasive fungal otitis externa (IFOE), particularly skull base osteomyelitis (MOE), is a devastating infection with high morbidity and mortality, often diagnosed at an advanced stage. Early, subclinical fungal invasion is difficult to detect on standard otoscopic examination, leading to diagnostic delays. An automated screening tool could enable earlier intervention, potentially preventing progression to MOE, especially in high-risk populations like diabetics and immunocompromised patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subtle, subclinical features of early fungal otitis externa, imperceptible to the human eye on standard video otoscopy, can be detected and quantified using a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on a curated dataset of otoscopic images and videos.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage deep learning model. First, a U-Net architecture will segment the tympanic membrane and external auditory canal from video otoscopy frames. Second, a DenseNet-121 classifier, pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned, will analyze the segmented regions for early fungal signs (e.g., subtle granulation, micro-ulceration, atypical debris). The model will be trained and validated on a retrospective dataset of labeled videos from patients with confirmed IFOE (various stages), bacterial otitis, and normal ears, using clinician diagnosis as the gold standard.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinician-assistive software tool for screening high-risk patients, aiming to reduce diagnostic latency. It will contribute a novel, annotated video otoscopy dataset for fungal ear disease. The project will establish a methodological framework for applying computer vision to subclinical otologic infection detection, a largely unexplored application.
proposals.novelty: While AI has been applied to otoscopy for diagnosing otitis media, its use for detecting early, subclinical *fungal* infections of the external canal is novel. The approach shifts the diagnostic paradigm from identifying advanced MOE to preventing it through pre-symptomatic screening, leveraging video data rather than static images.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Social Support as a Predictor of Cochlear Implant Success: A Retrospective Cohort Study of High-Performing Solo-Living Adults
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 clinical guidelines and research in cochlear implant (CI) rehabilitation often emphasize the necessity of a supportive cohabitant, potentially creating access barriers for single or solo-living adults. This assumption, while rooted in the need for post-operative support, lacks robust evidence and may overlook compensatory strategies developed by independent individuals. Challenging this paradigm is crucial for equitable patient selection and personalized rehabilitation planning.
proposals.hypothesis: Solo-living status is not a significant independent predictor of poor speech perception outcomes post-CI activation in adults. High-performing CI users living alone will demonstrate comparable auditory outcomes to their cohabiting counterparts when controlling for known confounders like age and duration of deafness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult CI recipients (n=200) from a tertiary care database (pre-2026). Participants will be stratified by living status (solo vs. cohabiting). Primary outcome: CNC word scores at 12 months post-activation. Secondary outcomes: AzBio sentences in noise and patient-reported outcomes (e.g., SSQ). We will employ multivariable linear regression, controlling for age, etiology, pre-op hearing, and CI experience. Subgroup analysis will identify characteristics (e.g., technology use patterns, pre-operative counseling) of high-performing solo users through structured chart review and a supplemental survey.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence to refine pre-CI counseling and candidacy assessments, reducing potential bias against solo-living candidates. It will identify the specific behavioral and technological strategies employed by successful independent users, informing the development of tailored, self-directed rehabilitation protocols. This shifts the focus from mandated social support to enabling patient self-efficacy.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges a long-held, untested clinical assumption about social support's necessity, moving beyond correlational studies to isolate the variable of living status. It leverages existing clinical data (inventory) and patient-reported measures to diagnose factors for success in an understudied population, aligning with Clark's themes of diagnosis and inventory in a chronic disease (hearing loss) context.

proposals.title: Pre-operative Emotional Intelligence Training and Its Effect on Cochlear Implant Speech Perception and Quality of Life Outcomes: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implant (CI) outcomes vary widely among recipients, and psychological readiness is increasingly recognized as a modifiable factor influencing post-operative rehabilitation success. Emotional intelligence (EI)—the capacity to perceive, regulate, and utilize emotions—has been linked to treatment adherence, coping with chronic conditions, and patient-clinician communication, yet its role in CI rehabilitation has not been formally investigated. Understanding whether targeted EI training can improve CI outcomes would offer a low-cost, scalable adjunct to existing audiological rehabilitation protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: Adults receiving structured pre-operative emotional intelligence training will demonstrate significantly greater improvements in open-set speech perception scores and self-reported quality of life at 6 and 12 months post-CI activation compared to a standard-of-care control group.
proposals.method: We propose a single-blind randomized controlled trial enrolling 80 adult CI candidates across two academic otolaryngology centers. The intervention group will receive a manualized 6-session pre-operative EI training program adapted from the RULER framework, targeting emotion recognition, emotional regulation, and empathic communication skills relevant to hearing rehabilitation. The control group will receive standard pre-operative counseling. Primary outcomes will be measured using AzBio sentence recognition in quiet and noise at 6 and 12 months post-activation, and the Nijmegen Cochlear Implant Questionnaire (NCIQ) for quality of life. Secondary outcomes include the Trait Emotional Intelligence Questionnaire (TEIQue), the Hearing Handicap Inventory for Adults (HHIA), and a validated patient activation measure. Mixed-effects linear models will compare trajectories between groups, adjusting for age, duration of deafness, and baseline EI scores.
proposals.contribution: This study would be the first to establish whether pre-operative psychological preparation targeting emotional intelligence can measurably improve CI rehabilitation outcomes. Positive findings would provide an evidence-based, cost-effective intervention that could be integrated into standard CI candidacy protocols, potentially reducing the variance in post-implantation performance and improving patient satisfaction.
proposals.novelty: While psychological counseling is sometimes offered to CI candidates, no prior study has specifically isolated emotional intelligence as a trainable construct influencing audiological outcomes. This approach bridges affective science and otologic rehabilitation in a way that challenges the predominantly techno-audiological framing of CI success predictors.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Multi-Center Study of Psychosocial and Quality-of-Life Outcomes in Pediatric Patients Receiving Cochlear Implants: The Role of Parental Support and Pre-Implant Infection History
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on cochlear implant (CI) outcomes often focuses on audiological metrics, with psychosocial factors like quality of life (QoL) and parental influence studied in smaller, retrospective cohorts. This limits causal inference and generalizability. Furthermore, a history of recurrent otologic infections (a known factor in CI candidacy) may independently affect psychosocial trajectories, but this interaction is poorly characterized in large-scale studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a large prospective cohort, superior longitudinal psychosocial outcomes in pediatric CI recipients will be significantly predicted by (1) higher baseline levels of structured parental support and (2) an absence of a significant pre-implant history of recurrent otitis media or related infections, after controlling for audiologic and surgical factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-year prospective cohort study across 5 tertiary otolaryngology centers, enrolling 300 pediatric CI candidates. Baseline data (pre-implant) will include a validated parental support inventory, detailed clinical history of infections (e.g., frequency, severity, treatments), and clinician-rated video assessments of child-parent interaction. Follow-ups at 6, 12, and 24 months post-activation will use standardized pediatric QoL and psychosocial functioning scales (e.g., PedsQL). Analysis will employ mixed-effects linear regression models to identify predictors of outcome trajectories, with specific attention to the interaction term between infection history and parental support scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, prospective evidence for modifiable psychosocial predictors of CI success beyond hearing alone, directly informing pre-implant counseling and family-centered care pathways. It will clarify the long-debated impact of pre-existing otologic disease on holistic outcomes, offering a more nuanced view for clinical decision-making. The large, multi-center design ensures findings are generalizable to diverse clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While parental factors and infection history are known independently, this study prospectively tests their interactive effect on psychosocial outcomes in a large cohort, moving beyond retrospective associations to establish temporal and potentially causal relationships critical for intervention design.

proposals.title: Predicting Cochlear Implant Rehabilitation Outcomes: A Supervised Learning Model Integrating Audiological, Psychosocial, and Demographic Features
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implant (CI) outcomes vary widely, and current pre-implant assessments are poor at predicting individual rehabilitation benefit, leading to suboptimal patient counseling and resource allocation. This gap mirrors challenges in managing chronic conditions like chronic rhinosinusitis, where heterogeneous patient factors influence treatment success. A predictive model would aid clinicians in setting realistic expectations and personalizing post-operative support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised machine learning model trained on pre-operative audiological, demographic, and psychosocial patient data can accurately predict post-CI rehabilitation outcomes, measured by standardized speech perception and quality-of-life metrics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult CI recipients, extracting pre-operative features (e.g., duration of deafness, residual hearing, cognitive screening scores, psychosocial inventories, demographics) and post-operative outcome labels (e.g., AZBio sentence scores, NCIQ questionnaire results at 12 months). We will apply and compare the performance of several pre-2026 supervised learning algorithms, including Random Forests, Gradient Boosting Machines (XGBoost), and regularized logistic regression, using nested cross-validation to prevent overfitting and identify the most predictive feature set.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, interpretable clinical decision support tool to forecast CI benefit, directly informing pre-operative counseling and rehabilitation planning. It will also identify novel, modifiable psychosocial factors influencing outcome, offering targets for pre-habilitation interventions to improve success rates.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to audiological data, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed psychosocial inventories and patient-reported measures—factors highlighted in chronic disease management—with traditional clinical metrics to build a holistic predictive model for a surgical hearing restoration intervention.

proposals.title: Parental Emotional Intelligence and Family Environment as Predictors of Cochlear Implant Outcomes in Pediatric Recipients: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implantation (CI) in children yields highly variable speech and language outcomes, and while device-related and audiological factors have been extensively studied, psychosocial determinants within the family remain poorly understood. Parental emotional intelligence (EI) — encompassing emotion regulation, empathic responsiveness, and stress management — likely shapes the quality of caregiver-child interaction critical for auditory-linguistic development post-implantation. Understanding these modifiable psychosocial factors could inform targeted family interventions that substantially improve CI outcomes in the pediatric population.
proposals.hypothesis: Higher parental emotional intelligence scores and more supportive family environments, as measured by validated psychometric instruments, will independently predict superior speech perception and expressive language outcomes in children at 12 and 24 months post-cochlear implantation, after controlling for age at implantation, residual hearing, and socioeconomic status.
proposals.method: This prospective cohort study will enroll 120 children (ages 1-6 years) receiving their first CI across three academic medical centers. At baseline, parental EI will be assessed using the Trait Emotional Intelligence Questionnaire (TEIQue), family environment via the Family Environment Scale (FES), and parental stress via the Parenting Stress Index (PSI-SF). Child outcomes will be measured at 12 and 24 months post-activation using the Infant-Toddler Meaningful Auditory Integration Scale (IT-MAIS), Preschool Language Scales (PLS-5), and open-set word recognition tests. Hierarchical multiple regression and structural equation modeling will be used to determine the independent and mediated contributions of parental EI and family environment to CI outcomes, controlling for established covariates including age at implantation, duration of deafness, communication mode, and socioeconomic indicators.
proposals.contribution: This study will be among the first to quantify the independent contribution of parental emotional intelligence to pediatric CI outcomes, shifting the clinical paradigm from a device-centered to a family-systems framework. The findings will provide an evidence base for developing pre-implantation psychosocial screening protocols and targeted caregiver EI training programs. Ultimately, identifying modifiable family-level predictors could reduce outcome variability and improve cost-effectiveness of pediatric cochlear implantation programs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined parental involvement and socioeconomic factors in CI outcomes, no study has specifically isolated parental emotional intelligence as a predictor variable using validated EI instruments. This inversion of focus — from child-centered audiological factors to caregiver affective competencies — represents a novel psychosocial lens that bridges otolaryngology with developmental psychology.

proposals.title: Psychosocial Predictors of Successful Hearing Aid Adoption: A Cross-Device Comparison with Cochlear Implant Users
proposals.motivation: While psychosocial determinants of cochlear implant outcomes have been increasingly studied, far less attention has been given to whether the same predictors—self-efficacy, social support, illness perception, and psychological distress—generalize to hearing aid users. Given that hearing aids remain the most commonly prescribed amplification device worldwide, understanding shared and divergent psychosocial pathways is critical for optimizing audiologic rehabilitation. Identifying generalizable predictors would enable clinicians to apply unified screening tools across amplification modalities, improving patient-centered care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core psychosocial constructs (self-efficacy, social support, and health locus of control) predict successful device adoption in hearing aid users with comparable effect sizes to those observed in cochlear implant populations, while device-specific factors such as cosmetic concern and perceived stigma will show differential predictive strength across groups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, cross-sectional study recruiting 150 adult hearing aid users (≥6 months post-fitting) and 150 cochlear implant users from two academic otolaryngology clinics. Participants will complete validated instruments including the General Self-Efficacy Scale (GSE), the Illness Perception Questionnaire-Revised (IPQ-R), the Multidimensional Scale of Perceived Social Support (MSPSS), and device-specific outcome measures (International Outcome Inventory for Hearing Aids, IOI-HA; Nijmegen Cochlear Implant Questionnaire, NCIQ). We will use hierarchical multiple regression to model device adoption success as a function of psychosocial predictors, controlling for audiometric severity, age, and duration of device use. Multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) will test measurement invariance and compare path coefficients across the two amplification groups, allowing formal assessment of whether predictor-outcome relationships are equivalent across devices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, within-framework comparison of psychosocial predictors across hearing aids and cochlear implants, establishing which factors are universal to amplification success and which are device-specific. The findings will inform the development of a streamlined psychosocial screening battery applicable across audiologic settings, reducing assessment burden while maintaining predictive validity. Results will also guide targeted pre-fitting counseling interventions for patients at risk of device abandonment.
proposals.novelty: Existing literature examines psychosocial factors for hearing aids and cochlear implants in isolation, using heterogeneous frameworks that preclude direct comparison. By applying an identical psychosocial determinant battery to both populations and using multi-group SEM to formally test cross-device generalizability, this study addresses a critical methodological gap that no prior work has systematically bridged.

proposals.title: Determinants of Cochlear Implant Outcomes in Low-Resource Settings: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Psychosocial and Environmental Barriers
proposals.motivation: Cochlear implant (CI) outcomes research is predominantly conducted in high-resource settings with robust rehabilitation infrastructure, creating a significant evidence gap. In low-resource healthcare settings, where access to post-operative auditory therapy and device support is severely limited, the relative impact of psychosocial factors (e.g., family support, parental education) and environmental determinants (e.g., device maintenance, travel burden to clinic) on functional hearing outcomes is poorly understood. This limits the development of effective, context-specific intervention strategies for global hearing health equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in low-resource settings, psychosocial and environmental determinants (e.g., caregiver literacy, distance to follow-up care, device troubleshooting access) will be stronger predictors of post-CI speech perception scores than traditional clinical-audiological factors alone, and that these determinants cluster into distinct, modifiable profiles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, longitudinal cohort study of 60 pediatric CI recipients and their parents at a tertiary center in a low-resource region. Quantitative data (speech perception scores, inventory of device use, validated scales for family stress and social support) will be collected at 6, 12, and 24 months post-activation. Concurrently, semi-structured video interviews with parents and clinicians will explore barriers to rehabilitation. Analysis will employ a mixed-effects regression model to identify outcome predictors, followed by k-means clustering (using Euclidean distance on standardized psychosocial/environmental variables) to identify distinct patient/family profiles. Qualitative data will be analyzed via thematic analysis to contextualize quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated model of CI outcome determinants specific to low-resource contexts, moving beyond a purely biomedical framework. It will yield a validated inventory of context-specific barriers and produce actionable profiles to guide targeted, resource-efficient support interventions, such as tailored parent training modules or decentralized follow-up protocols using telehealth.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents CI access disparities, this study uniquely applies a mixed-methods, determinant-mapping approach to outcomes *after* implantation in resource-constrained environments, directly linking quantitative outcome data with rich qualitative insights from parents and frontline clinicians.

ideas.title: Causal inference framework for odontogenic CRS in elderly populations using instrumental variables and negative control designs
ideas.description: This study implements a multi-site prospective cohort of elderly adults (≥65 years) with deep comorbidity phenotyping, using regional dental insurance coverage and water fluoridation policies as instrumental variables to estimate the causal effect of dental health deterioration on CRS incidence while circumventing the pervasive confounding by shared risk factors in geriatric populations. The design incorporates negative control outcomes (e.g., hearing loss) that share confounders with CRS but have no plausible dental pathway, allowing quantitative bias analysis to detect and calibrate residual confounding. Dental exposures are measured via standardized periodontal and radiographic assessments at baseline and 12-month intervals, with CRS outcomes adjudicated by CT imaging and EPOS criteria, enabling both time-to-event and mediation analyses that decompose direct odontogenic pathways from systemic inflammatory mechanisms. This causal inference architecture transforms an otherwise uninterpretable correlational study into a robust platform for estimating population-attributable fractions and informing preventive dental screening guidelines in geriatric care.

ideas.title: Hearing loss cascade is unidirectional

ideas.sourceTitle: Cochlear Implant Rehabilitation in Older Adults: Literature Review and Proposal of a Conceptual Framework

ideas.title: Prevalence and diagnostic accuracy of odontalgia for detecting odontogenic sinusitis in refractory CRS: a prospective blinded cohort study
ideas.description: This prospective cohort study enrolls consecutive patients with chronic rhinosinusitis refractory to medical therapy and performs standardized dental pain assessments alongside cone-beam CT imaging, with blinded independent adjudication of odontogenic etiology by both an otolaryngologist and an endodontist. By calculating the sensitivity, specificity, and negative predictive value of odontalgia as a screening tool for odontogenic CRS across a large unselected cohort, the study directly quantifies how often painless dental pathology is missed and whether absence of tooth pain can reliably exclude dental origin. This design overcomes the fatal limitation of anecdotal case evidence by providing population-level prevalence data and actionable diagnostic accuracy metrics that could justify routine dental imaging in CRS workups.

ideas.title: Long-term cosmetic and functional outcome comparison
ideas.description: Prospective assessment of long-term scar satisfaction, voice outcomes, and recurrence rates comparing both transoral approaches.
ideas.sourceTitle: Early outcomes in transoral vestibular thyroidectomy: Robotic versus endoscopic techniques

ideas.title: Hidden odontogenic etiology as an uncontrolled confounder in head and neck outcome studies
ideas.description: Head and neck cancer outcome studies that match controls by tumor site, T-category, and age assume the local tissue environment is comparable, yet unrecognized odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis—which can present without any dental pain—introduces a hidden confounder that these matching schemes never capture. Patients harboring silent dental-origin sinus inflammation may exhibit altered mucosal immunity, shifted microbial flora, and different postoperative complication profiles, systematically biasing survival and recurrence analyses in ways that overlap with and obscure HPV-status effects. This fused research direction proposes a prospective cohort framework in which sinonasal CT and dental evaluation are performed on all head and neck cancer subjects at enrollment, enabling quantification of occult odontogenic disease prevalence and its independent effect on treatment outcomes after adjusting for HPV, comorbidities, and conventional matching variables. The approach uniquely bridges diagnostic reasoning from rhinology with methodological rigor in oncologic epidemiology, potentially reclassifying a subset of unexplained outcome variance in existing matched-cohort studies.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: CI preventing cognitive decline
ideas.description: Investigate the inverse question of whether early CI intervention can prevent or slow age-related cognitive decline.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cochlear Implant Rehabilitation in Older Adults: Literature Review and Proposal of a Conceptual Framework

ideas.title: Oral Language as Primary Success Metric
ideas.description: Challenge the implicit privileging of spoken language over sign language as the benchmark for successful development.
ideas.sourceTitle: Timing of Cochlear Implantation and Parents’ Global Ratings of Children’s Health and Development

ideas.title: De-escalated Adjuvant Therapy Guided by Geriatric Dental Health Risk Stratification in Oropharyngeal Cancer
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a prospective cohort study in elderly patients (e.g., >70 years) with HPV-associated OPSCC treated with TORS. It integrates large-scale, pre-treatment dental health/oral chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) screening to stratify patients based on their baseline inflammatory/immunosenescence profile. The core hypothesis is that patients with superior dental/periodontal health (low inflammatory burden) represent a biologically favorable subgroup in whom de-escalated adjuvant therapy (e.g., reduced-dose radiotherapy alone) will yield equivalent oncologic outcomes to standard chemoradiation, while potentially minimizing toxicity. This moves beyond anatomic or clinical staging alone by adding a geriatric-specific biological risk modifier to personalize de-escalation candidacy.

ideas.title: Lifespan cohort study of odontogenic sinusitis from deciduous teeth to edentulous aging
ideas.description: This fused research direction proposes a multi-generational, population-level cohort framework that tracks odontogenic sinusitis risk across the entire human lifespan, from deciduous tooth infections in early childhood through progressive dental deterioration in geriatric populations. By establishing linked pediatric and elderly cohorts within the same population registries, the study can investigate whether childhood odontogenic sinus events—often underdiagnosed and undertreated—alter maxillary sinus anatomy, mucosal immunity, or microbial colonization patterns in ways that elevate chronic rhinosinusitis risk in later life. The approach leverages large-scale dental and ENT health records to build age-stratified risk models that capture critical transitional periods (mixed dentition, full permanent dentition, tooth loss, and edentulism) as predictors of sinus disease. This lifespan epidemiological perspective transcends what either pediatric-focused or geriatric-focused studies can achieve independently, potentially identifying early-life dental intervention windows that reduce sinusitis burden decades later.

ideas.title: Prospective OCRS prevalence study with dental screening
ideas.description: Conduct prospective studies systematically screening all CRS patients with dental examination to determine true OCRS prevalence beyond the estimated 10-30%.
ideas.sourceTitle: Chronic Rhinosinusitis of Odontogenic Origin.

ideas.title: Dual: Predicting Treatment Outcomes from Otoscopy
ideas.description: Extend from diagnosis to prognosis by predicting treatment response or surgical outcomes from baseline otoscopic images.
ideas.sourceTitle: Artificial intelligence to diagnose ear disease using otoscopic image analysis: a review

ideas.title: Staged escalation protocol for odontogenic sinusitis: prospective identification of predictors for endoscopic sinus surgery after dental treatment failure
ideas.description: This prospective cohort study enrolls patients with confirmed odontogenic sinusitis into a standardized staged-treatment protocol where all patients first undergo definitive dental treatment (extraction or endodontic therapy) and are reassessed at 3 months using validated symptom scores (SNOT-22), cone-beam CT opacification grading, and nasal endoscopy. Patients meeting pre-registered failure criteria are escalated to endoscopic sinus surgery, creating a within-cohort design that eliminates selection bias inherent in retrospective dental-versus-combined comparisons. The primary outcomes are the proportion requiring surgical escalation and 12-month sinusitis resolution rates across both responder groups, while secondary analyses build a multivariable prediction model incorporating baseline features (duration of dental pathology, degree of sinus opacification, presence of fungal elements, oroantral communication size) to identify which patients benefit from early combined intervention. This design directly addresses confounding by indication, produces actionable clinical decision rules, and establishes the true incremental value of sinus surgery beyond adequate dental treatment.

ideas.title: Few-Shot Learning for Rare Ear Pathologies
ideas.description: Apply few-shot or meta-learning methods from computer vision to diagnose rare ear conditions with limited training examples.
ideas.sourceTitle: Artificial intelligence to diagnose ear disease using otoscopic image analysis: a review

ideas.title: Prospective integrated ENT-dental screening trial to establish evidence-based diagnostic pathways for odontogenic CRS
ideas.description: Conduct a multicenter prospective trial in which all patients presenting with chronic rhinosinusitis undergo systematic dental examination and cone-beam CT as part of a standardized ENT-dental diagnostic pathway, simultaneously generating robust OCRS prevalence data and validating the pathway's diagnostic accuracy. By embedding the prevalence study within a structured clinical pathway, the trial produces both the epidemiological evidence (true OCRS prevalence stratified by laterality, antibiotic refractoriness, and dental risk factors) and the clinical validation data (sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value of pathway triggers) needed to refine referral criteria. This fusion enables a feedback loop: early prevalence findings inform iterative pathway refinement during the trial, yielding an evidence-calibrated guideline specifying which CRS subpopulations benefit most from mandatory dental evaluation. The resulting pathway would be the first to ground its screening recommendations in prospectively derived prevalence and diagnostic performance data rather than retrospective case series.

ideas.title: Item Response Theory for Rating Scales
ideas.description: Use IRT models from psychometrics to improve measurement precision of parental global health and development ratings.
ideas.sourceTitle: Timing of Cochlear Implantation and Parents’ Global Ratings of Children’s Health and Development

ideas.title: Predictive modeling of odontogenic sinusitis risk in elderly populations using oral-health and demographic features
ideas.description: This fused approach builds large geriatric cohort datasets that systematically link dental health indicators (periodontal status, edentulism, periapical pathology, implant history) with chronic rhinosinusitis incidence, then applies the same predictive modeling and feature-engineering pipelines developed for TORS patient selection in oncology informatics to construct validated risk-stratification models for odontogenic CRS. The ML framework would identify which combinations of oral-health, comorbidity, and demographic features most strongly predict progression to clinically significant sinusitis, enabling targeted screening in high-risk elderly subgroups rather than relying on universal or symptom-triggered evaluation. By adapting supervised classification and survival-analysis techniques proven in surgical oncology triage to the dental-sinus disease axis, the approach yields a deployable clinical decision-support tool that triages elderly patients for early ENT referral based on routine dental examination data — a capability neither population epidemiology nor oncology-focused ML achieves independently.

ideas.title: Attrition-robust longitudinal swallowing surveillance using hybrid remote-clinic monitoring and competing-risk analysis
ideas.description: This study establishes a multi-year swallowing outcome surveillance system that overcomes the critical limitation of survivor bias in long-term dysphagia research by combining periodic in-clinic videofluoroscopic and endoscopic evaluations with monthly automated digital patient-reported outcome collection and caregiver-proxy surveys for cognitively impaired or homebound participants. Linkage to national death registries and hospital discharge databases enables real-time tracking of competing events including mortality, aspiration pneumonia admissions, and feeding tube placement. Statistical analysis employs joint longitudinal-survival models and inverse-probability-of-censoring weighting to produce unbiased estimates of swallowing function trajectories over 24-36 months, explicitly modeling the relationship between dropout patterns and dysphagia severity. This design yields the first methodologically rigorous characterization of late-onset dysphagia incidence and long-term functional recovery that accounts for the full spectrum of patient outcomes rather than only accessible survivors.

ideas.title: A multicenter study correlating TORS surgical volume with instrumental swallowing outcomes to define volume-outcome benchmarks
ideas.description: This study proposes a prospective, multicenter investigation that mandates uniform instrumental swallowing assessment (FEES or videofluoroscopy) at standardized post-operative intervals across institutions with varying TORS surgical volumes. By collecting high-fidelity, objective swallowing data in a harmonized protocol, the research will directly correlate institutional and surgeon volume with specific, quantifiable functional deficits and recovery trajectories. This will allow for the identification of critical volume thresholds necessary to achieve optimal, instrumentally-verified functional outcomes, creating a data-driven framework for defining center-of-excellence criteria and surgical training requirements. The synergy lies in using standardized assessment to transform volume from a crude proxy into a precise predictor of physiological recovery, enabling the development of validated benchmarks for healthcare policy and credentialing.

ideas.title: Borrowing from pediatric UTI screening protocols to detect odontogenic sinusitis from deciduous teeth
ideas.description: Pediatric odontogenic sinusitis from deciduous teeth mirrors the historical challenge of pediatric UTIs: both are conditions well-recognized in adults but systematically underdiagnosed in children due to nonspecific symptomatology, low clinical suspicion, and the assumption that the pediatric anatomy is less susceptible. In pediatric urology, this diagnostic gap was closed through the development of evidence-based screening guidelines that lowered the threshold for testing in at-risk populations (febrile children without a clear source), used accessible diagnostic tools, and established age-specific decision rules. Adapting this approach, we propose a prospective multicenter study to develop and validate a clinical prediction rule for odontogenic sinusitis in children aged 4-12 with deciduous or mixed dentition. The protocol would enroll children presenting with chronic or recurrent sinusitis unresponsive to standard medical therapy, systematically evaluate dental pathology (periapical radiolucencies, deep caries in deciduous molars adjacent to the maxillary sinus floor), and correlate findings with limited-field cone-beam CT imaging of the sinuses. By establishing prevalence estimates, identifying key clinical and radiographic predictors, and creating a validated screening instrument, this work would parallel the paradigm shift achieved in pediatric UTI detection—transforming an overlooked etiology into a routinely screened condition in pediatric ENT and dental practice.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Survey Methods to Triangulate Swallowing Outcomes After TORS
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that patient-reported swallowing outcomes accurately reflect objective function after Transoral Robotic Surgery (TORS). Drawing an analogy from ecology, where scientists routinely reconcile discrepancies between field observer reports and instrumental sensor data, the solution is adapted. In ecology, integrated models calibrate subjective data against objective benchmarks. The adapted research idea is to move beyond simple correlation studies. It proposes developing a multivariate calibration model that uses core objective instrumental findings (e.g., penetration-aspiration scale, pharyngeal constriction ratio from videofluoroscopy) to identify and correct systematic biases in patient-reported outcome (PRO) measures. The goal is to determine for which functional domains (e.g., liquid vs. solid swallowing) and which patient phenotypes (e.g., based on tumor location, adjuvant therapy) the PRO-instrumental discordance is greatest, and to produce a validated correction algorithm. This would allow clinicians and researchers to better interpret PRO scores by understanding their calibrated relationship to physiological function, improving clinical decision-making and the validity of outcome studies.

ideas.title: Scale Down to Single-Pathology Severity Grading
ideas.description: Instead of multi-class disease detection, scale down to fine-grained severity grading within a single condition like otitis media.
ideas.sourceTitle: Artificial intelligence to diagnose ear disease using otoscopic image analysis: a review

ideas.title: Systematic dual-organ screening for hidden comorbidity prevalence: Adapting celiac disease screening paradigms to odontogenic CRS
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from the transformation in celiac disease epidemiology, where prospective population-based serological screening revealed that the true prevalence was vastly underestimated when diagnosis relied solely on symptomatic presentation and clinician-initiated testing. The 'iceberg model' of celiac disease demonstrated that for every diagnosed case, multiple undiagnosed cases existed in the community. Similarly, odontogenic chronic rhinosinusitis (OCRS) prevalence estimates of 10-30% likely suffer from referral and detection bias, as dental causes are only investigated when explicitly suspected. The adapted study design would mandate standardized dual-modality screening—cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) of the maxillary dentition plus comprehensive periodontal examination—for every consecutive CRS patient presenting to participating rhinology centers over a defined enrollment period, mirroring how celiac screening studies tested all patients in gastroenterology cohorts with serological panels. Predefined radiographic and clinical criteria for dental pathology (periapical lesions, oroantral communications, periodontal disease extending to the sinus floor) would be applied uniformly. This protocol eliminates the ascertainment bias inherent in retrospective studies and symptom-triggered dental referrals, enabling construction of an OCRS prevalence iceberg model that distinguishes clinically apparent from screening-detected odontogenic contributions. The multi-center design, analogous to the large celiac seroprevalence studies, would also permit analysis of geographic, demographic, and dental-health-system factors influencing OCRS detection rates.

ideas.title: Comprehensive geriatric assessment for CI candidacy
ideas.description: Apply comprehensive geriatric assessment methodology from geriatric medicine to evaluate CI candidacy and predict outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cochlear Implant Rehabilitation in Older Adults: Literature Review and Proposal of a Conceptual Framework

ideas.title: OCRS limited to maxillary sinus involvement
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that odontogenic sinusitis stays localized to the maxillary sinus, given documented frontal and ethmoid extension.
ideas.sourceTitle: Chronic Rhinosinusitis of Odontogenic Origin.

ideas.title: Long-Term Outcomes Beyond Twelve Months
ideas.description: Evaluate whether trismus improvements from early Restorabite therapy persist beyond the reported twelve-month follow-up period.
ideas.sourceTitle: Traumatic Facial Nerve Neuroma With Facial Palsy Presenting in Infancy

ideas.title: Inverse problem: protective ear canal microbiome
ideas.description: Investigate which ear canal microbiome compositions prevent Aspergillus colonization, inverting the infection pathogenesis question.
ideas.sourceTitle: Temporal Bone Histopathology Case of the Month Malignant Otitis Externa Caused by Aspergillus fumigatus: A Case Report

ideas.title: Long-term dysphagia trajectories as a model for understanding persistent bacterial source effects on chronic treatment outcomes
ideas.description: This fused research direction investigates how unresolved upstream pathological sources—such as persistent dental infection seeding in odontogenic CRS or unaddressed tissue damage leading to late-onset dysphagia—produce misleading short-term treatment success that masks long-term functional decline. By extending follow-up beyond 12 months in both head-and-neck swallowing cohorts and CRS patients treated with antibiotics alone versus source-elimination surgery, the study creates a comparative longitudinal framework that captures delayed relapse and late-onset deterioration across two distinct clinical domains. The central hypothesis is that interventions failing to address the root anatomical or infectious source will show diverging outcome trajectories after 12 months, with initially equivalent outcomes giving way to significantly worse function in the symptom-only treatment arm. This cross-domain design yields generalizable evidence for why long-term follow-up and source-directed treatment are jointly necessary to avoid underestimating treatment failure in chronic head-and-neck conditions.

ideas.title: CBCT-guided treatment stratification for odontogenic sinusitis: predicting surgical necessity from pre-operative imaging
ideas.description: This study develops a CBCT-based predictive model that stratifies odontogenic sinusitis patients into those likely to resolve with dental treatment alone versus those requiring combined dental extraction and endoscopic sinus surgery. Pre-operative cone-beam CT scans—already acquired during dental implant or extraction planning—are analyzed for quantitative sinus features including mucosal thickening volume, ostiomeatal complex patency, and extent of dental pathology penetration into the sinus floor, then correlated with long-term sinusitis resolution outcomes. The fusion creates a clinical decision support tool that neither idea achieves independently: CBCT imaging alone cannot predict treatment outcomes without outcome data, and outcome studies alone cannot identify pre-operative imaging biomarkers without standardized volumetric imaging. The resulting algorithm would enable personalized treatment planning, sparing low-risk patients from unnecessary surgery while ensuring timely surgical referral for high-risk cases, all using low-dose imaging already part of the dental workflow.

ideas.title: Living alone may not impair CI outcomes
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that cohabitation is necessary for successful rehabilitation by studying high-performing solo-living CI users.
ideas.sourceTitle: Rehabilitation and Psychosocial Determinants of Cochlear Implant Outcomes in Older Adults

ideas.description: Incorporate child-reported quality of life measures alongside parental global ratings to capture divergent perspectives.
ideas.sourceTitle: Timing of Cochlear Implantation and Parents’ Global Ratings of Children’s Health and Development

ideas.sourceTitle: Cochlear Implant Rehabilitation in Older Adults: Literature Review and Proposal of a Conceptual Framework
